entries.prompt: What's something you said yes to recently that you weren't sure about — walk me through the moment you agreed, and what was going through your head afterward.
entries.content:
Mei asked me on Thursday if I could take the lead on the stakeholder deck for next week's review. She phrased it the way she always phrases things — not quite a question, more like an observation being made at me: "I think you'd be really good at owning this one." And I heard myself say, "Yeah, absolutely, I'd love to," with my face doing something warm and willing, and the whole transaction was over in about four seconds.

She smiled and said great and walked back to her desk and that was that.

I sat there for a moment afterward with my hands in my lap under my desk where she couldn't see them, and I was squeezing them together in a way I didn't notice until I felt my knuckles. Because what was actually going through my head, under the yeah absolutely I'd love to, was: I have no idea how to structure this for that audience. And: I'm already behind on the two things I moved to accommodate the sprint review. And, quieter and less reasonable: why does it always land here.

Why does it always land here isn't even a sensible thought. I'm good at decks. I'm good at synthesis and I know the project better than most people. The ask was not unfair. But there's that other, smaller voice — *you said yes because you can't stand for Mei to think you're the kind of person who says no* — and that voice is the one that's probably closer to true.

I went to get more coffee after, which I didn't need, just to be doing something. Stood in the kitchen for a minute looking at the window above the sink. The city does this thing in October where the light turns flat and gold in the afternoons and it was doing that then. I thought: if Devon had been the one to ask me, I probably would have said let me look at my week first. I would have bought myself twenty-four hours to decide. With Mei I don't do that. With Mei I just give the answer I want her to think I'd give, and then I figure it out later.

I went home and didn't mention it to Sam right away. We made dinner, we talked about his thing with the leaking faucet and whether to call the landlord or try to fix it, normal stuff. Then later I was at the table with my laptop and he came up behind me and glanced at the screen — which was a blank presentation file with the title placeholder still saying "Click to add title" — and he said "new project?" and I said "kind of, I said I'd do this deck and now I have to actually do it."

He made a small sound, not judgmental, just — acknowledging. The sound he makes when he has already clocked something I haven't fully admitted yet.

"Was it something you wanted to do?"

I said yeah, sure, I think it'll be good for me to lead something like this. Which is true. It probably will be. But sitting there with the blank slide looking at me, that's not actually what I was feeling. What I was feeling was something more like: this is mine now and I can't give it back and I have to make it good or she'll know you can't really do this and this is what I get for saying yes when I meant maybe.

*This is what I get.* Like there was a punishment coming, when the only person who made the decision was me.

Sam wandered off eventually. I stared at the blank file for a while longer and then I opened a browser and started reading about how other people structure stakeholder reviews, which isn't really working on the deck, it's researching working on the deck, which is its own form of delay. I know that. I did it anyway.

The weird thing is, I also know it won't be that bad. I've done things like this before and they've gone fine, usually better than fine, and the version of me who will be standing in that room next week with a clicker in her hand will look totally calm because she will have practiced being calm until it became indistinguishable from actually being calm. That part I've gotten good at. The part I haven't gotten good at is the twelve days before, when the dread is just sitting in the corner of everything.

I texted Riley something unrelated tonight. Just a link to a thing I thought she'd find funny. She sent back three words and a laughing emoji and that was the whole conversation. There's a real conversation I've been meaning to have with her — not about the deck, she doesn't know about any of this — just a longer one, a phone call, checking in properly. We keep saying we'll do it and then we text links at each other instead. It's easier. I don't know if easier is good or just comfortable.

I eventually did make a start on the deck. One slide with a rough outline of sections. It looked thin but it was something. I closed the laptop after that because it was past eleven and I knew the looking-at-it-longer wasn't going to produce more, it was just going to make the dread more specific.

I keep coming back to the moment in the kitchen with the gold light and the window. What it would have cost me to say to Mei, *can I have a day to look at my plate?* Probably nothing. She's direct but she's not punitive. The catastrophe I'm defending against — the one where she thinks less of me for having limits — is almost entirely mine. I made it. I've been maintaining it for two years. 

And still when she looks at me and says I think you'd be good at this, I feel something that is not quite pride and not quite panic, and whatever it is, it makes my mouth say yeah absolutely before my brain has any say in the matter.

I'll do the deck. It'll probably be fine. She'll probably be pleased.

I just want to know when I started treating other people's good opinion of me like a thing I have to keep paying for, or else it goes away.

entries.prompt: What did you say to yourself the last time something didn't go the way you wanted — walk me through the moment it went sideways and what the voice in your head said right after.
entries.content:
The deck review happened yesterday. It went fine — that's the accurate, factual word for it. Fine. People nodded. Mei said "good framing" at one point, which is the kind of thing she says when she's satisfied but not surprised. I had practiced the transitions between slides until I'd stopped hearing the words as words, just sounds in the right order. The clicker felt familiar in my hand. I answered the two hard questions without audibly hesitating.

Afterward Devon caught me in the hall and said "you killed it" and I said something like "team effort, the material was already there" and he waved that off and I was glad he waved it off.

So where did it go sideways? It didn't, not visibly. That's the part I'm still working on explaining to myself.

There's a slide near the end — the one about the roadmap dependencies — where I'd made a judgment call to simplify. The actual situation is more tangled than the slide made it look, and I knew that, and I made the call that this audience didn't need every thread, they needed the shape of it. That's a legitimate editorial decision. I made it deliberately. I still made it. And when we got to that slide and one of the senior stakeholders — a guy who only shows up for these things twice a year — glanced at it and moved on without a question, I felt, for a fraction of a second, relieved.

And then: *oh.*

The thought that came right after the relief was: *you smoothed over the complicated part and they didn't notice, which means you got away with it, which means you should feel good, except you don't, you feel like you just lied to someone and they believed you.*

That's not a fair characterization of what happened. I know that. I stand by the simplification. It was a good call. But the voice doesn't do fair characterizations — it does first reactions, and the first reaction was: *this is the thing you're actually good at. Not the synthesis, not the framing, not any of the stuff Mei said good job on. You're good at making complicated things look less complicated so that no one asks the question that would require you to know more than you do. This is a performance and you have been performing.*

I was at the front of the room when that thought happened. Still talking, I think. The mouth doesn't stop just because the voice starts. I've learned to run them in parallel.

I don't actually believe I'm only a performance. I want to say that clearly, here, to myself, because otherwise it sits there and calcifies into something I'm afraid to look at. I know things. I did the work on that deck. The simplification wasn't evasion, it was craft. But knowing that and feeling it at the same time, in the same moment — I'm not there yet. The knowledge is in one room and the voice is in another and they don't consult each other.

The real sideways part came later, actually.

We were in the small debrief afterward, just Mei and Devon and me and one other person from the project. Loose conversation, nothing formal. And Mei said something about the next phase and how she was thinking about who should own the narrative going forward, and she looked — not at me, she looked around the room generally, the kind of looking that isn't asking anyone specifically — and Devon said something and I said something and the conversation moved and nothing was decided.

But I had looked at her when she said it. I had looked at her the way you look at someone when you want them to look back.

She didn't.

And then the voice, the real version, in the two seconds after:

*See. She thought it was fine too. Fine. That's the ceiling. You did it well enough that it satisfied the requirement and she's already looking ahead at the next thing and it won't be you. It's never actually going to be you, you understand that, right? You can do every deck, you can say yes to everything she phrases as an observation at you, and you will be the person she thinks is reliable and that's different from the person she thinks is— *

I didn't let it finish the sentence. Which is maybe its own kind of problem.

I went back to my desk and sat in my chair for a minute and just looked at the screen without doing anything. Devon came by and said something easy, I can't even remember what, and I responded normally because by then I was running both tracks again, the normal outside one and the quieter internal one, and they don't interfere with each other, they've been roommates too long.

I walked home instead of taking the train. It's about twenty-five minutes if you go the long way, and the October light was doing its thing again, and I had my hands in my pockets and I was trying to just be someone walking home instead of someone running a loop about what Mei's eye contact did or didn't mean.

It helped a little. The walking always helps a little.

Sam asked how the presentation went and I said fine, really fine, I think people were happy with it. Which is true. He said "good, I knew it would be" and I said "did you" in a tone that was almost teasing and he laughed a little. It was a warm couple of minutes. I was glad to be home.

Then later I was in the bathroom brushing my teeth and the voice came back in a quieter form, which is almost worse because the quieter form sounds more like just — thinking, rather than the panicked catastrophizing I can dismiss as irrational. The quiet version said: *the thing you want her to think about you, you might not actually be able to make someone think that. You can do the work and do it well and they still locate you where they locate you. That's possible. That's a real thing that happens.*

And I didn't have a response to that one. I just looked at my own face for a second, toothbrush in hand, and then I spit and rinsed and turned the light off.

I don't know what I'm defending against exactly. Being good but not exceptional. Being trusted but not chosen. There's a difference between those things, I think, and I don't talk about it to anyone — not Sam, definitely not Mei, not even Riley, who I still haven't called. Some things I write down here instead and then I turn the page and I keep going.

It went fine. The deck went fine. She probably doesn't think about it the way I think about it. That's both the reassuring version of events and the one that keeps me up.

entries.prompt: Describe a moment recently when you were alone and realized you'd been performing for someone who wasn't even there anymore — what were you doing, and who were you playing to?
entries.content:
Saturday morning. Sam was out — he'd gone to pick up a few things, he said maybe an hour, it was one of those casual mornings where neither of us had anywhere specific to be. I made a second cup of coffee and sat at the kitchen table with a book I've been slowly working through for about six weeks. The apartment was quiet. The good kind of quiet.

I read three pages. Then I put the book face-down on the table and just sat there, and I noticed after a moment that I was sitting up quite straight. Shoulders back. The posture I have at work, in meetings, when someone might be watching how I'm holding myself. I was alone in my own kitchen in a sweatshirt I've had since college, and I was sitting like I was about to present something.

I don't know how long I'd been doing it.

I made myself slouch — not dramatically, just normally, the way you sit when no one is performing — and it felt almost physically strange. Like I'd been wearing the meeting posture for so long that the other version of my body had become the costume and the upright version had become the default.

Who was I playing to? That's the question I've been turning over since.

The honest answer is Mei, but it's not really Mei. It's some version of Mei that I've built and installed somewhere behind my sternum, and that version watches things. She watches me read. She watches how quickly I respond to messages. She watched me last week on the walk home after the presentation, checking whether I was processing the experience efficiently or just moping. I don't think she has opinions about my posture at the kitchen table, the real Mei. I don't think she thinks about me on Saturday mornings. But the installed version does, and she has very consistent standards.

There was a moment, maybe fifteen minutes after I noticed the posture thing, where I picked up my phone and almost texted Devon — something about a project tangent we'd talked about briefly on Friday. A follow-up, something that would make it look like I'd spent part of the weekend thinking productively. I had the message half-composed in my head. And then I just — didn't. I put the phone down without opening it. Because I realized the text was not for Devon, it was for the version of me that Mei would think well of, and Devon was just the available character in that scenario.

*You were going to manufacture evidence of yourself working on a Saturday,* the voice said, *because you can't sit in your own kitchen for twenty minutes without needing someone to know you're still trying.*

That one landed. I'm not going to dress it up.

I stayed at the table for a while after that. The coffee was getting cold. I started reading again and this time I actually read — five or six pages, taking it in rather than processing the physical fact of the words. There's a thing that happens sometimes when I manage to stop performing even internally, where actual time passes in a way I can feel. Like the difference between looking at a view and taking a picture of a view. Somewhere in the middle of a paragraph I realized I'd been reading for real for maybe ten minutes and it was fine. Nothing had collapsed.

The thing I'm trying to figure out is when it started. The installed version of Mei, I mean. Because I've had it for longer than I've known her. There was a version of it in my first job, calibrated to a different face. There was a version of it in college, calibrated to a professor, a roommate, someone who seemed to know how to be serious and competent in a way I was actively figuring out how to imitate. The names and faces update. The structure stays.

I know what I'd say if someone else described this to me. I'd say, that sounds exhausting, I'd say, you must be really hard on yourself. And they'd say yeah, and I'd probably know what to say next. But knowing what to say and being able to apply it to yourself are so far apart sometimes that the gap is almost funny. Almost.

Sam came back with the groceries and I helped put them away and we talked about what to do with the afternoon — a walk maybe, or just staying in, we weren't sure. Normal weekend negotiation. He made a joke about something and I laughed, a real laugh, not the managed kind, and I noticed I was leaning against the counter with my arms crossed loosely and the posture was gone entirely, I'd forgotten to maintain it.

That's the other version. That's what the kitchen is supposed to feel like.

He asked if I'd gotten any reading done and I said a little, I was slow-going with this one. He said he'd seen me at the table with it earlier. I said yeah. He opened his mouth like he was going to say something, then reconsidered, then got a glass of water. He does that sometimes — opens a door and then lets me decide whether to walk through it.

I thought about walking through it. Trying to explain the posture thing, the Mei thing, the almost-unnecessary text to Devon. But explaining it requires believing I could explain it in a way that makes sense, and I wasn't quite there.

So I just said, "it's a good book, it's just slow."

He said "fair enough" and that was that.

I'm trying to understand what the performing is actually for. Not the surface version — I know the surface version, it's about approval, it's about Mei's opinion, this is not new information about myself. I mean under that. What I seem to believe will happen if I stop. If I sit in my kitchen like a person who is not waiting to be evaluated. Something apparently terrible, given how consistently I resist it. Something so bad that even alone, on a quiet Saturday, with a book and cold coffee, I'll hold my body like a proof of something.

I kept reading after the groceries were put away. Not efficiently. Not toward anything. Just continuing the story at the speed it wanted to go.

That's probably what I'll remember about today. Not the posture thing, not the almost-text. Just the part where I kept reading.

entries.prompt: Think about someone in your life you've been keeping a certain distance from lately — not dramatically, just… less contact than there used to be. When did you last have a real conversation with them, and how did you leave things?
entries.content:
Riley.

I've been thinking about Riley all week and I haven't done anything about it. The last real conversation — and I mean a real one, on the phone, where you can hear someone's actual voice and there are pauses that mean something — was probably four or five months ago. I'm almost sure it was before the summer. We talked about her apartment situation, which was uncertain then, and about a trip I was considering that didn't end up happening. I remember walking around the block while we talked because I was inside and felt restless and it was one of those calls where forty minutes passed without either of us noticing. We said we'd talk again soon. That was the four or five months ago.

Since then: texts. Links to things. A voice memo once that was about thirty seconds long and mostly just her laughing at something she'd seen on the street. I sent back a string of punctuation and an emoji and she responded in kind and that was apparently enough to constitute contact. We're fine. That's the thing — we're genuinely fine, in the way that the deck went genuinely fine. The category is technically correct and doesn't capture anything.

There's a specific conversation I've been avoiding and I didn't fully understand that until just now.

Last fall, before the winter, Riley mentioned — not heavily, she mentioned it the way she mentions most things, which is sideways and then moving on — that she was going through something with her job, a situation where she'd been passed over for something she'd been counting on and hadn't told many people she was counting on it and so the disappointment was sort of private and therefore somehow worse. She said a version of this. She said it briefly. And I said the right things, I know I did, I said god that's awful and I hear you on the keeping it private thing, that makes it stranger somehow.

And then the conversation moved on because she let it move on, and I let her let it.

And I haven't asked her about it since. Not once. Not in the texts, not in the voice memo exchange, not in any of the eleven or twelve thin little threads we've called keeping in touch. I don't know if it resolved, if she got something else, if it still sits somewhere in her chest the way the Mei thing sits in mine. I don't know because I didn't ask. I told myself it was because she'd moved on, she was fine, she would have brought it back up if she wanted to talk about it.

But I think the real reason is this: if I asked her about it properly, I'd have to stay on the phone long enough for her to ask how I was in return. And if she asked how I was in the real way she asks — not the how are you that expects fine, the version she's had since college where the word *how* has actual weight in it — I don't know that I could keep the surface clean. I'd want to tell her about the deck and about Mei and about sitting alone in my kitchen practicing good posture for an audience of no one. And once I start talking like that it tends to go in a direction I can't control. That's what good friends do. That's the part I've been avoiding.

So I keep sending links to funny things. Which is love, some kind of love, but it's easy love. It costs nothing. The distance it maintains is mutual but I suspect I'm the one who set it.

I thought about calling her this weekend. I actually picked up the phone on Sunday morning and found her name and stayed on the screen for probably a full minute. Sam was in the shower, I could hear the water running. I thought: the call would be so easy. She'd pick up and say oh my god I was just thinking about you, because she always says that, because we have whatever frequency that means we tend to think about each other right before the other one surfaces. She'd say it and I'd feel immediately like it was a Thursday afternoon in our mid-twenties and the world was moderately comprehensible.

I put the phone down.

Told myself it was the wrong time of day, she might be busy, I'd text first to see if she had a few minutes.

I have not texted.

There's something I keep almost-thinking and then going around the edge of. Here it is: the version of me that Riley knows best is from before I got so concerned with being the person Mei thinks is capable. Riley knew me before the professional shape calcified. Before the instinct to say yeah absolutely I'd love to became the first reflex. She'd recognize what I was describing right away, and she'd probably call it something true and a little unsparing, the way she does, and then she'd ask what I actually wanted to do about it rather than just observing it here in my own handwriting where no one can push back.

I think that's what I'm avoiding. Not the conversation. The moment after, where I can't just sit with it anymore.

I'm going to call her this week. I'm not going to write the exact day because I've done that before and the specific day becomes an object I can either accomplish or fail to accomplish and it stops being about Riley and starts being about whether I follow through on things I say to myself. Which is a whole other conversation.

But this week. Before it's six months.

I want to know about the job thing. I want to know if it still bothers her or if she found a way to let it go, because she's sometimes better at that than I am and I'd like to study it. I want to do the forty-minutes-without-noticing thing again. I want her to use my name the way she does when she's being serious about something, which is slightly different from how everyone else says it — a little slower, a little more like she's picking it up and looking at it.

I miss her in that very quiet way where you don't realize how much until you actually write it down.

The cold coffee thing, the window above the sink, the October light going flat and gold. I keep noticing those moments alone and not telling anyone about them. That's what the phone call is for, I think. It's for saying *the strangest thing happened, I was just sitting there* and having someone who knows exactly what kind of person you were at twenty-three say yeah, that sounds like you, tell me more.

I owe her that call. I mean: I want to make it. Those are different things and I'm trying to use the right one.

entries.prompt: When's the last time you made a decision that you knew would disappoint someone — and didn't walk it back? Walk me through what happened.
entries.content:
I called Riley on Tuesday.

That's not what this prompt is asking about, but I need to say it first, that I called her and she picked up on the second ring and said *oh my god, I was literally about to text you* and I laughed in a way that came out slightly wet because I hadn't known I was anywhere near that until I heard her voice. We talked for fifty minutes. She asked how I was in the real way, with the weight in it, and I told her about Mei and the deck and the kitchen posture thing. She listened without performing listening, which is its own skill. She said, *you know that's not new, right, you've always done this thing where you decide in advance what the worst version of other people's opinions is and then you work backwards from there.* I said yeah, I know. She said: *does knowing help.* I said: not as much as you'd think.

She's still a little raw about the job thing, the passed-over thing from last fall. She said it in a way that was casual but I could hear the edge under it. I told her I'd been bad about following up and she said, honestly, *you disappeared a little.* Not accusing, just accurate. I said I know, I'm sorry. She said don't be sorry, just don't do it again. That's her, that's how she handles things, and I took it in as the gift it was.

So. That happened.

What this is actually about is Wednesday. The day after.

My mom called in the evening, one of the weekly-ish calls, and she asked if I'd thought about coming home for a long weekend in November. She'd been looking at dates, she said. She mentioned a specific weekend — the one around a holiday, the obvious one — and she'd already checked whether the train would be manageable.

I knew as soon as she said it that I wasn't going to go. Not because I don't want to see them — I do, genuinely — but because I have known for two weeks that November is going to be a difficult month at work, the kind where you have to be findable, and also because I'm already tired in a way that traveling into my parents' particular atmosphere tends to intensify rather than relieve. That's the true thing. My parents are fine. My mom asking slightly pointed questions about whether I'm sleeping enough is not cruelty, it's something closer to love without good aim, and I know that. But a long weekend there, right now, would not be restorative.

I usually handle this by saying *I'll look at my calendar and let you know.* Which means I go away and I agonize about whether I'm a bad daughter and then I either cave and book the ticket or I put it off long enough that the specific dates become irrelevant and we find another weekend four months out and by then I've extended the guilt almost to the point of meaninglessness.

This time I said: "Mom, I don't think I can do that particular weekend. Work is going to be a lot in November."

She was quiet for a moment in the way she does, not a hostile silence, a thinking silence, and then she said, "you always say that about work."

And I said, "I know. But it's true this time." And then I said we could figure out December, I wanted to see them, I didn't want to go a long stretch without visiting.

She said okay. She didn't entirely believe me, I could hear that, the slight flattening of her tone. She pivoted to asking about Sam and whether we'd gotten the faucet situation sorted out. I said yes. We talked for another fifteen minutes about normal things and then we said goodbye and I love you and she said take care of yourself, which is her way of saying she worries.

I held the phone for a second after the call ended and waited for the feeling I usually get, which is something like having just violated an agreement I didn't agree to. The weight of having let her down, which would normally send me straight to the calendar to find a way to walk it back.

It didn't come. Not the full version.

There was something — a smaller version of it, a little flicker of *maybe I should have just said yes.* I won't pretend it was clean. But it was a flicker instead of a current, which is different, and I just let it flicker and then I put the phone down and went and made tea.

Sam was on the couch reading and he looked up and said "your mom?" and I said yeah, she wanted me to come home for that November weekend and I said I couldn't. He said "oh yeah?" in a careful way, because he knows my history with this particular type of conversation.

I said, "I didn't walk it back. I just said no."

He put his book down. Not dramatically — he folded the corner of the page, which I usually tease him about, and he looked at me for a moment. And he said, "good."

One word. I thought I might want more than one word, but actually one was right.

I don't know why Wednesday felt different from the usual pattern. Maybe it was the Riley call the night before, having said the true things out loud to someone who knew what to do with them. Maybe it's something more gradual, something that's been accumulating. Maybe I was just tired enough that the reflex didn't have energy to fire. I can't attribute it cleanly to growth or progress or anything with a shape. It just happened differently this time.

My mom wasn't devastated. She'll be fine. She'll ask about December when December gets closer and I'll probably go and we'll have a weekend that is mostly good with some moments that aren't and that's what visiting family is. I didn't break anything by saying no.

The thing I keep noticing is how quickly I braced for impact that didn't arrive. Like there's an old recording somewhere that plays *this is the part where it gets bad* and the recording played and then nothing happened and I was just standing in the kitchen holding my phone, and the tea kettle was starting to make its sound, and the city outside the window was doing its nighttime version of itself, and everything was ordinary in the best way.

I want to remember that. The bracing and then the nothing. The nothing being the whole lesson.

entries.prompt: What's something you've wanted to say to Sam lately that you haven't said — walk me through the last moment you almost brought it up.
entries.content:
Last night we were doing the dishes together. It's become a thing we do when we're both home for dinner — he washes, I dry, it's not a rule, it's just what happens. There's something I like about it. The domesticity of it, the slight inefficiency, the way a conversation can go in and out of silence without it meaning anything.

He was telling me about a conflict with someone at his job, low-stakes, the kind of workplace friction that's annoying and will probably resolve itself. I was drying the same bowl for too long, going around and around the rim, half-listening, thinking about something else, and then he said something that made me laugh and I came back into the room and that was that.

But for a minute there, while he was talking, I had been somewhere else entirely. I had been thinking about a conversation I had with Devon yesterday afternoon. 

Devon mentioned, in the way Devon mentions things — sideways, not trying to make anything of it — that there's a structural conversation happening at the level above us. About next year. About who's going to take on certain kinds of work as some things shift around. He wasn't telling me anything specific, he probably didn't know anything specific, but the shape of what he was describing was: things are being decided, and people are being thought about, or not being thought about, and at some point the deciding will be done.

And I had stood there in the hallway with my coffee and said something like *huh, interesting, thanks for the heads up* and I had felt, under that, the beginning of a particular kind of dread. Not fear exactly. More like: I know this feeling, this is the one where I start performing at a higher frequency for an audience that may not even be watching.

While Sam was talking about his thing with the dishes, what I wanted to say was: I'm worried I'm not going to be one of the people they think about. 

I almost said it. I turned it over a few times, figured out the first sentence, the one that would open the door. I've been thinking about something, or: Devon said something yesterday. Either would have worked. He would have put down the sponge and turned toward me and the conversation would have started.

I didn't start it.

Partly because he was in the middle of his own thing and it wasn't the right moment, which is true and also a useful piece of timing to hide behind.

But the real reason, if I'm being accurate about it: I've already told him so much of this. The Mei thing, the deck, the way I sit up straight when no one's watching. He has a whole portfolio now of my professional anxieties, cross-referenced and filed. He holds it well, he's never made me feel like I'm being tedious or circular, but I still feel the accumulation. Like at some point the reasonable response to *I'm afraid I'm not going to be chosen for something I haven't been offered and don't know the exact nature of* is going to be: you've been afraid of some version of that for two years. What do you actually want to do about it?

Which is a fair question. It's the same question Riley asked — does knowing help — and I didn't have a better answer then either. 

I think what I actually want, when I imagine starting the conversation with Sam, is not advice. Not even the quiet *good* he gave me after the phone call with my mom, which was perfect for that moment. What I want is for him to look worried on my behalf for a second. Just briefly. I want someone to register that the stakes feel real to me, not to validate that they are real — I'm not sure they are, in any measurable sense — but to acknowledge the feeling has some weight to it.

And I'm not sure I know how to ask for that specifically. *Can you just be briefly worried with me* is not a natural sentence. So instead I dry the bowl in circles and stay inside it.

He noticed something, I think. Not the Devon thing, he doesn't know about that. But later, when we were on the couch, he did the thing where he reaches over and just rests his hand on my ankle for a moment — not a big gesture, just a contact, checking in. I covered his hand with mine for a second and he went back to whatever he was reading. That was the whole thing.

It's possible that's enough. It's possible the conversation is happening in a language that doesn't require the words and I'm the one who keeps insisting words are the only version that counts.

But there's another part of it I haven't gotten to yet, the part I like even less.

Under the worry about being overlooked is something quieter and a little harder to look at directly. Which is that I don't know, specifically, what I want. Not in a lost or directionless way, not like the floor is gone — more like, if someone sat me down and said: what would the right outcome look like for you, a year from now, tell me what you're actually hoping for — I'm not sure I could answer with a sentence that felt true instead of correct. I have the correct answers ready. They're about growth and doing meaningful work and being trusted with harder problems. They're not lies. But they're also a kind of performance of knowing what you want, rather than actually knowing.

Sam would ask the right version of that question. That's part of why I didn't start the conversation. Because if he asked it and I gave the correct answer and he looked at me in that patient way he has, the one that means he knows the difference between the answer and the real answer, I'd have to sit with not knowing in front of someone else. Which is worse than sitting with it here.

Riley asked me, after I'd told her about Mei and the deck and the posture thing: *what do you actually want from Mei? Like, if you could just have the thing, what would it be?* And I said I wanted her to think I was exceptional. Riley said: *and then what.* 

I didn't have an answer. We moved on. But I've been thinking about it since. And then what. If I got the thing, if Mei looked at me and saw whatever version of me I'm performing toward, and then what. I don't actually know. I'm not sure the goal has ever had a second step.

Sam is a second step person. He's naturally comfortable in the and-then-what. I think that's part of why I love him and also, lately, part of why I'll dry the bowl in circles for three minutes and say nothing.

We went to bed at a reasonable hour, which is rare for me lately. He was asleep fast, the way he does, like sleeping is just a thing he does rather than a thing he has to negotiate. I lay there for a while in the dark with the ceiling and the orange-grey of the city coming through the curtain.

I thought: I could wake him up and say *hey, I have a thing I've been carrying.* He would want me to. That's not a guess.

I didn't wake him up. But I did just lie there, without my phone, without reviewing anything. The ceiling doesn't have opinions about me. It was actually pretty restful.

I'll tell him something true tonight, I think. Not necessarily that specific thing. Just — something with a second step in it.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been meaning to do for someone else — not a big gesture, just a small thing — that you keep not getting around to? Walk me through the last time it crossed your mind and what you did instead.
entries.content:
Devon's birthday is sometime this month. I don't know the exact date — I've never asked and he's never made anything of it — but I know it's October because last year he mentioned something offhand about getting older right as the weather was turning, the first genuinely cold week, and I filed it away in the approximate way you file things that don't feel urgent.

I've been meaning to do something small. Not a big thing. Not even a card, really — just one of those moments where you acknowledge someone in a specific way, where you say I actually paid attention to that thing you said eleven months ago. Devon is easy to be around and I take that easiness for granted more than I should. It costs him something to be that way. Or maybe it doesn't, maybe it's just how he's made, but either way I want to mark the thing, do the small gesture.

Last week I was in a shop near work, one of those places that sells nice notebooks and expensive pens and cards that cost six dollars but feel worth it. I went in because I needed tape — we'd run out at home and I'd been carrying that on a to-do somewhere in my head for days. And while I was standing there waiting for the guy at the register to deal with someone's return, I found myself in the card section, and I thought: Devon. October. Do this now. I was right there. I could have had the card and a stamp in my bag in four minutes.

I read about twelve cards. I put two back. I picked up one with a design that seemed like him — something clean, a little dry, not aggressively heartfelt — and then I thought: I don't even know if his birthday has happened yet. If I time this wrong it'll be weird, it'll require explanation, it'll be more about me having done the thing than about the birthday. I put the card back. I got my tape. I left.

This was last Thursday.

I thought about it again Monday, in the way you think about things you've already half-abandoned — not with urgency, more like: that's still there. Devon and I ate lunch together, just the two of us, at the long table by the windows. He was talking about a project he's mildly dreading, I was listening and asking follow-up questions and it was one of those easy lunches where an hour goes by and it doesn't feel like work. On the walk back to the office I thought: I could just ask him. I could say when's your birthday, in a casual way, people ask that. It's not weird.

I didn't ask. The moment when it would have been casual passed and then it felt like asking would make it strange, would make it a thing I was clearly following up on having thought about and then I'd have to explain.

So I know his birthday is this month and I still don't know exactly when, and the card is still in the shop presumably, and I am doing the thing I do where a small gesture becomes oddly load-bearing and then collapses under the weight of me overthinking the logistics.

The embarrassing part — and I'm writing this because I want to see it clearly, not because I'm proud of it — is that I think the hesitation isn't purely logistical. There's something underneath it. Devon is easy, Devon doesn't grade me, I relax around Devon in a way that's different from how I am with Mei. And there's a small, not-great part of me that treats that ease like it's a given, like it doesn't need to be maintained or nourished, because Devon will be there either way. Mei is the one I perform for. Devon is the one I take for granted. I know which of those is worse, actually, and it's not the performance.

If I sent him a card he'd probably make a joke about it. He'd bring it up for weeks in a cheerful, slightly deflating way — he'd show other people probably, say something like look, she sent me an actual card, do you know anyone who sends cards anymore. And that's fine. That's good, even. That's Devon. But I would have had to let the gesture exist in the world without being able to control how it lands, and apparently I haven't been ready to do that.

I walked to work this morning the long way, because the weather finally broke overnight and it's the first really good October morning, the kind where the air has some clarity in it and you want to be outside for reasons beyond transit. I went past the shop with the cards. I didn't stop.

I thought about stopping.

The thing is: if Riley had mentioned a birthday of someone she cared about and then described this same sequence to me — the card, the almost, the tape, the lunch, the follow-up thought, the not-asking — I know exactly what I'd say. I'd say: just go back and get the card, it doesn't have to be perfectly timed, the gesture is the point. I'd say: Devon sounds like someone who'd love that you remembered, logistics aside.

I'd say all of that without hesitation and it would be true.

The gap between what I know and what I do with that knowledge is something I keep running into from different angles and it's always the same gap, just with different furniture in it.

I'm going to get the card today at lunch. That's not a resolution I'm writing down to feel the satisfaction of resolution — I actually am going to do it, I'm going to walk over there and pick the dry funny one and not overthink the timing and just put it in his cubby or whatever you'd call the spot where we leave things for each other. If his birthday has already passed, it passed, it can be a late one. Devon will make a joke and I'll let him make the joke.

I think I've been waiting to do small things for people until I can do them perfectly. Until the timing is right and the gesture reads exactly the way I intend and the landing is controlled. Which means the small things don't happen, and the people I like the most — the ones I'm easiest around, the ones who don't require performance — are the ones who get the least from me because they're not going to say anything about it.

Mei gets the deck, prepared and practiced and revised. Devon gets the approximate month when I sort of almost did something.

That's not a characterization I want to sit with for very long without doing something about it.

Lunch. The shop. The clean, slightly dry card. And then whatever happens happens.

entries.prompt: What's something you're better at than you let on — and when did you last downplay it in front of someone?
entries.content:
I got the card, for the record. Devon opened it at his desk and immediately held it up to show the person across from him — I could see him from where I was sitting — and he looked over at me with an expression that was about seventy percent genuine pleasure and thirty percent already working on what to say about it, and he said *nobody sends cards anymore, this is a historical artifact, I'm going to have it framed.* I said I'd found it in a museum. He laughed. It was, start to finish, about forty-five seconds. I've been thinking about those forty-five seconds more than I'd like to admit because there was nothing complicated in them. Just a thing that worked.

---

The downplaying thing. I've been sitting with this one all day.

There's a meeting we have on Thursdays where a small cross-functional group goes through whatever's tangled up at the intersection of multiple projects — handoffs that aren't clean, dependencies that are vague, the places where two teams think the other team is handling it. It's not glamorous. Nobody leaves those meetings feeling like they've built something. You mostly leave feeling like you've prevented a specific small collapse.

I'm good at those meetings. I mean actually good, not fine-and-probably-good-enough. Something happens for me in rooms like that — I can hold six threads at once, I can feel where the tension is between two people before they've said anything pointed, and I can usually find the reframe that lets everyone feel like they got something rather than gave something up. I've been told this. Not by Mei, specifically — or not often by Mei — but by people who are in those rooms with me, including senior people, people who've been doing this longer than I have.

Today after the Thursday meeting, one of the people who runs a team adjacent to ours — someone I don't work with directly, I barely know her — stopped me in the hall and said, without preamble: *you're really good at running those.* Just that. She had somewhere to be, she didn't elaborate, she kept walking.

And I said: *oh, it's a pretty small group, it makes it easier.*

I heard myself say it. I watched myself sand it down in real time, take the compliment and return it smaller, attributed to circumstances rather than to anything I did. She was already gone by the time I finished the sentence.

The thing is: the group size is irrelevant. I've been in small groups that were a disaster. The size didn't make the meeting easy. I made the meeting run the way it did and I know that, and I deflected anyway, reflexively, the way you pull your hand back from something hot before you've consciously processed what you're doing.

I went back to my desk and thought about why.

The honest version is layered and I want to try to get all the way down.

The first layer: modesty, social reflex, the not-wanting-to-seem-like-you-think-too-much-of-yourself. That's the least interesting layer. Everybody has it to some degree. That's not what this is.

The second layer: something specific to how I've built things with Mei. If I'm good at the facilitation work, the navigating-complexity work, the making-people-feel-heard work — and I accept that I'm genuinely good at it — then there's a natural question about why that skill set hasn't translated into being the person Mei thinks of as exceptional. And I don't want to look at that question too directly, because if the answer is that I am, in fact, quite capable and just not being recognized in the way I want to be recognized, then the situation becomes more uncomfortable, not less. It would require me to stop laying the problem at the feet of my own inadequacy and start looking at other explanations. That's scarier, somehow.

I've spent two years being available to the hypothesis that I'm not quite enough. It's a familiar shape. Uncomfortable, but familiar. The other hypothesis — that I'm doing real work well and the recognition is just running on a different timetable, or calibrated to different things, or more about visibility than capability — that one requires me to want something more specifically and be more directly disappointed when I don't get it. The inadequacy story keeps me safely vague.

The third layer is the smallest and the hardest to write down.

I think part of me is afraid that if I acknowledge what I'm good at, I'm also agreeing to be accountable for it. Like if I say yes, I'm good at this, then I've made a claim that can be tested and disproven. Right now I can hedge. I can say I think it went okay, I think it was useful, the circumstances helped. That keeps the worst version away — the version where I believed in myself and then turned out to be wrong. Saying yeah, actually, I'm good at this, that's mine — that's a different kind of exposure.

Riley would probably identify this immediately. She'd say: you're allowed to have skills you're confident about, those two things aren't in conflict. And she'd be right. And I'd know she was right. And it would still take me a while to let it in.

The walk home was good today. The morning clarity had held, which October doesn't always allow — usually you get the good light in the morning and by afternoon the whole thing has flattened out, but today it stayed. I went the long way again, past the park, and there were leaves coming down in the kind of slow, floating way that feels arranged, even though it's just wind and physics.

I thought about the woman in the hallway. Her matter-of-factness about it. No preamble, no qualifying. Just: *you're really good at running those.* And then she left, because she wasn't making a big thing of it and didn't need me to either. She was just saying a true thing in passing.

I let myself, on the walk, not diminish it. Not perform gratitude about it or immediately wonder what Mei thinks or start calculating whether it's the kind of capability that translates. Just: someone said a true thing and maybe it is true.

I held that for about a block and a half, which is actually pretty good for me.

Sam will probably ask about my day tonight and I might tell him this one. Not the layers, not the analysis — just the story. The woman in the hall, the thing I said back, the way I heard myself deflect in real time. He won't be surprised by any of it, but that's not the point. The point is I want to say out loud: I think I'm good at that particular thing. I want to hear how it sounds coming out of my mouth in a room with another person in it, rather than here where it's just me and the page and no one to push back.

I want to practice saying it like I mean it. The forty-five seconds with Devon taught me something: the thing you've been overthinking almost always takes less time than you thought, and lands cleaner, and the world stays intact.

So. Tonight, probably. The true version.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been looking forward to lately — and when did you last catch yourself talking yourself out of it?
entries.content:
There's a show at the small venue near the river — the one with the exposed brick and the sound system that's better than it has any right to be for a room that size. A band I've liked for a few years, the kind of music that doesn't have a clean genre, which usually means the audience is small and specific and everyone there actually wants to be there. Tickets have been on sale for weeks. I found out about the show the day tickets went up, which almost never happens to me, I'm usually the person who finds out after the fact and feels the small grief of that. This time I knew. The date is a Saturday in November, which is a reasonable time for a thing to happen.

I haven't bought the ticket.

The talking-out-of-it has gone through several distinct phases, and I want to see if I can name them because I think they're each doing something different.

Phase one was logistical. November is going to be a hard month at work — I said this to my mom, I meant it, I stand by it. A Saturday show means staying out late and Sunday will feel compressed. The show is at a venue that requires some transit-planning. These are real considerations and also not actually prohibitive for a person who is physically fine and has done this many times.

Phase two was the companion question. I'd want to go with someone. Sam will come to most things if I ask but he doesn't love that kind of music — he'd go for me, and I'd know he was going for me, and I'd spend part of the show aware of that awareness, and the enjoyment would get split with the monitoring. So, someone else. Devon might be into it. Riley, obviously, would be into it — she introduced me to this band, actually, that's almost funny, she'd be delighted and she's also in another city. I thought about asking Devon and then thought about the logistics of asking Devon outside of work context, which is easy, we've done it before, but I told myself I didn't know his taste well enough, which is not entirely true. I've been talking myself around in circles about this for two weeks, finding a different logistical objection each time the previous one resolves.

Phase three is where it gets less comfortable to look at.

The show is on a Saturday in November and I could go alone. I've gone to things alone before and it's been fine, it's been more than fine — there's a specific freedom in it, arriving when you want, standing where you want, not managing anyone else's experience. I know this. But when I imagine actually buying one ticket, standing at the counter on the website, choosing my spot in the general admission area, I feel something I don't have a great word for. Something adjacent to exposure. Like attending a thing alone is a statement about the attendance. Like someone would look at me there and see not just a person who came to hear music she likes but something more diagnostic: the kind of person who comes to things alone.

Which is not a kind I think badly of. When I see someone at a show alone I think: they just wanted to hear the music, good for them. I think that and I mean it and I apparently can't apply it to myself.

I caught myself in this loop last Sunday and the loop itself annoyed me. I said, internally, in the less patient register: *you are thirty-one years old and you are telling yourself a story about what it means to stand in a room with music you like. The band isn't going to think less of you. The audience isn't watching.* And then more quietly: *and if they are, what?*

I didn't have a follow-up to that.

The thing underneath all of it — and I've been circling this since I started writing — is that the show represents a version of wanting something that is purely for me, and I've gotten oddly suspicious of those lately. The deck was for work. The phone call with Riley was for us, the relationship. The card for Devon was for Devon. Even the walks have some functional logic: they clear my head, they're good for me, there's an output. The show is for no one. I would go and hear music and come home and there would be no professional benefit, no relationship maintained, no productivity unlocked. It would just be a thing I wanted to do because I wanted to do it. And apparently I've been treating that as a harder sell than it should be.

Sam did ask about my day on Thursday and I told him the hallway story, the woman who said the true thing, the way I heard myself diminish it immediately. He listened. He said: "why do you do that?" Not accusatory, genuinely curious. I said I wasn't totally sure. He said: "you do it with the music thing too." I had told him briefly, weeks ago, that the show was coming up. I hadn't mentioned since then that I still hadn't bought a ticket. He had apparently been tracking it.

I said: I've been overthinking the logistics.

He looked at me. He has a specific look that is not the patient look, it's a slightly more direct look, the one where he's not going to make me say it but he's also not going to pretend he doesn't know what's happening. He said: "do you want to go to the show."

I said yes.

He said: "then buy the ticket."

I said it's a little complicated. He said: "I know you think that."

We left it there. He wasn't being short with me, it was almost warm, the way you can have a conversation about something in seven sentences and have it contain all the necessary information without either person having to perform understanding.

I thought about it again Tuesday night. Got as far as opening the ticketing website, looking at the remaining availability, which is fine — still enough space, still the kind of room it was always going to be. I looked at it for a minute and then I closed the tab and checked something else I can't even remember now.

So: I haven't bought the ticket. The show is in three weeks. Tickets are still available. I have no good reason not to go.

The Devon thing — the forty-five seconds, the thing that worked — I keep coming back to that. The card sat in my head as a logistics problem for weeks and then it wasn't. I walked over at lunch and picked it up and that was the whole thing, and it was just forty-five seconds of something clean. The gap between thinking about doing a thing and doing the thing is almost always smaller than the thinking makes it feel. I know this. I've tested it.

Tonight when I get home I'm going to open the website and I'm going to buy the ticket before I make coffee. Not after. Before, so I can't use the small transition ritual to delay.

One ticket. General admission. I'll stand somewhere with a good sightline and hear the music and come home. Sam will ask how it was and I'll tell him. Maybe I'll even tell him while it's still ringing a little in my ears, still present, before I've processed it into something reportable.

I want that. Just that. I'm allowed to want just that.

entries.prompt: Write about a moment recently when you were in the middle of something ordinary — a commute, a meal, a routine errand — and a thought showed up that you didn't expect. What were you doing, and where did your mind go?
entries.content:
I bought the ticket. That's settled. It's on my phone, the little confirmation email, the one with the QR code I'll inevitably panic about finding at the door. Three weeks, Saturday. That part's done.

This isn't about that.

Wednesday morning, the train. I was standing — it was crowded enough that I didn't get a seat, which is usually the thing that slightly ruins my morning, the forty-minute commute I can't use because I'm holding a strap and trying not to be too close to someone else's breakfast — and I had my headphones in but nothing was playing. I do that sometimes. The headphones without the sound, so the ambient noise comes in smaller and I have some notional privacy. I was watching the stops go by and thinking about nothing in particular, the train just doing its thing underground.

We were between stations, the section of track where the tunnel wall is close enough that you could touch it through the window and the whole car gets slightly louder, and I had this thought that arrived without any lead-up:

*I don't know what I'm building toward.*

That's it. That's the whole thought. I wasn't thinking about work, I wasn't thinking about Mei or the structural conversation Devon mentioned, I wasn't even thinking about the show. I was looking at the dark tunnel wall through scratched train glass and that sentence just showed up, the way sounds sometimes resolve suddenly out of static.

I didn't feel bad when it happened. That's the part I keep returning to. I expected to feel bad — it's not a comfortable thought, it implies a question I haven't been answering — but what I actually felt was something closer to recognition. Like: oh, there you are. Like the thought had been there for a while, patient, waiting for a moment when I wasn't performing at anything.

The train pulled into the next station and people shifted around me and I got a seat, which normally would have been the thing I noticed, the small improvement to my morning. I barely registered it. I sat down and kept thinking.

I have goals, technically. The correct-answer ones. I know what to say when someone asks, and I've said those things enough times that they've developed a kind of solidity that might be mistaken for belief. I want to take on harder work. I want to be trusted with more complexity. I want Mei to look at me in the next-phase conversation and think: her. Those things are all real, as far as they go. But they're all means. None of them are ends. If I got all of them — if Mei looked at me that way, if the structural conversation resolved in my favor, if the next year went the way the correct-answer version of me would plan it — I don't know what would actually be different. I don't know what I would feel, standing in that hypothetical future, that I don't already feel on a good week.

Riley's question, the one she asked on the phone and that I've been carrying: *and then what.* I said I didn't have an answer. I'm starting to think the not-having is the answer.

I didn't think this through on the train, it's not like the commute resolved anything. It just — opened something. I sat with it for a few stops and then the podcaster in the seat across from me was laughing loudly at something on his phone and the thought dissolved and I was back in the ordinary train car with my bag on my lap.

But I kept returning to it through the day, in the small gaps. Getting coffee. Walking back from the Thursday meeting, which Mei wasn't in but which I ran the way I always run it, smoothing the same places, holding the same threads. Eating lunch at my desk because I had too much on, the kind of eating where you're three bites in before you've tasted anything.

There's something I built without meaning to build it — a whole structure of effort and performance and wanting-to-be-seen, very solid, quite well-constructed, and I've been living inside it long enough that I stopped noticing it was a structure and started thinking it was just the shape of things. And then on a Wednesday morning between stations the thought walked in and said: *what's this for.*

Not a despairing question. That's what I want to be clear about, at least to myself. It didn't feel like crisis or collapse. It felt more like — noticing a sound in the apartment you've been tuning out for so long you forgot you were tuning it out. You can't unhear it now, but that's different from the sound being terrible.

I told Sam some version of this over dinner. Not the full version, I'm not sure I had the full version yet. I said something like: I had a weird moment on the train, I was just standing there and I suddenly thought I don't really know what I want the point of everything to be. He stopped eating for a moment. He said: *everything like work, or everything like—* and he made a small gesture with his fork that somehow indicated the scope of the question without finishing the sentence.

I said: work, mostly. I think just work. For now.

He nodded. He said he had that sometimes. He said it usually happened when he was in the middle of something that was fine but not interesting, and the not-interesting let something else in. I thought that was probably right. The commute, the strap-holding, the tunnel wall. Nothing to perform at.

He asked if it felt bad.

I said: not really. Mostly just honest.

He said: *that seems like the right response to an honest thought.*

Which is a Sam thing to say, plainspoken in a way that makes it sound obvious in retrospect and wasn't obvious before he said it. I've been thinking about it since. The right response to an honest thought. Like the goal isn't to resolve the thought, get past it, convert it into a plan — just to let it have been honest.

I went to bed at a decent hour and I didn't lie there reviewing anything. The ceiling, the orange-grey coming through the curtain, the city doing its nighttime hum. The thought was still there in the background, not pressing, just present. It's still there now.

I'm thirty-one and I don't know what I'm building toward. That used to be the kind of thing I'd experience as a failure, a thing I should have figured out already, evidence of some essential incompleteness. Sitting here tonight it just seems like information. True, and not finished, and mine to keep thinking about.

The show is in three weeks. There's that.

entries.prompt: What's something you used to want badly that you don't think about anymore — when did you last notice it was gone?
entries.content:
There used to be a thing I wanted — I haven't thought about it for a while and then this afternoon it walked back in without warning, the way things do when you've stopped expecting them.

I used to want to write something. Not a journal, not this — something made, something with a shape. A novel is the honest word for it, though I said "fiction" when I told people, the way you hedge the larger claim with the smaller one. I wanted to write a novel. I had a notebook in my mid-twenties — a thick one, dark blue cover, I think I still have it somewhere in the closet under some other things — where I was working on something. Characters. A situation. Scenes that sometimes went for three or four pages before I ran out of whatever had started them.

I was serious about it. That's the part I tend to forget. I wasn't a person who vaguely wanted to write someday. I had the notebook. I got up early on weekends to work in it. I told Riley and she asked me questions about the characters like they were real people she might meet, which is the thing you do when you take someone's work seriously, and I would answer her and sometimes the answering made me understand the thing I was doing better than the doing had.

Somewhere in the move to this city, or the job after that, or the relationship settling into its good and comfortable shape — somewhere in the accumulation of those things, the notebook went into the closet and I stopped getting up early and I started calling it something I'd get back to, and then I stopped calling it anything because you can only defer a thing so long before you start to forget it was ever due.

This afternoon I was cleaning out the desk — not a real purge, just the kind of restless tidying that happens when you're between tasks and not willing to start the next one — and I found a pen I recognized. It had been in the blue notebook, I was sure of it, one of those fine-point ones I bought a whole pack of specifically for that. I turned it over in my hands and thought: huh.

The thought after that, the one that came before I could manage it: *you had something you were working on and you let it go and you didn't even really decide to, you just kept putting it down and eventually it was down for good.*

I sat at the desk for a minute with the pen. The apartment was quiet, Sam was out somewhere, the afternoon light was doing its low October thing through the window.

The voice that came wasn't the usual harsh one. It wasn't *you failed at this, you didn't have what it took, you should have known you weren't really a person who does that kind of thing.* I was waiting for that one and it didn't arrive. What came instead was quieter and less armored: I just felt the shape of the absence. The way you'd find a groove in a table where something used to sit, something you'd stopped registering wasn't there.

I don't know when I last thought about it. A year, maybe more. There was a period where I'd occasionally mention it in the category of things I meant to return to — the creative thing, the writing thing — and then even that stopped. The forwarding address expired. I've been walking around without it for long enough that I genuinely didn't notice until the pen.

The question I'm sitting with is whether I still want it, or whether what I'm feeling is closer to grief for a version of myself who wanted it. Those are different. The grief version is real but it's backward-facing, it's about the mid-twenties person getting up early on Saturday with the thick notebook, and I can mourn that without it meaning I need to find the notebook in the closet tonight. But the other version — if I still want it, if the want is still current and not just memorial — that means something different. That means I've been walking around this whole time with a want I've been too busy, too tired, too performing-at-other-things to acknowledge.

I think it might be the second one. I don't know for certain. But when I imagine sitting down with something to write — not this, not the professional document, not the deck — I feel something that isn't just nostalgia. It has some heat in it. That might mean something.

The train thought from Wednesday is connected, I think. I don't know what I'm building toward. What I was building toward, once, was the novel. I had a destination that was mine, that had nothing to do with Mei, that no stakeholder had asked for, that would not demonstrate capability or warrant good framing. It was just a thing I wanted to make because I wanted to make it. And I set it down and I picked up all the other things, the career shape, the performance structure, the long intricate work of being the person who does the deck and says yeah absolutely, and at some point those things filled the space and I stopped noticing the gap.

Riley would have something to say about this. She knew about the notebook. She asked about the characters like they were real. I didn't mention it on the phone last week — it didn't come up — but she'd remember it if I brought it back. She'd say: *you knew this.* Not unkindly. Just the way she says things that are true.

I didn't go looking for the notebook tonight. I thought about it and then I didn't, and I'm trying to sit with whether that was avoidance or just being gentle with the thing, giving it some air before I go pulling it out into the light. The closet can hold it a little longer.

But I put the pen on the desk, not back in the drawer. That's something. Small, maybe too small to mean anything, but I didn't put it back.

Sam came home and I was still at the desk and he said something easy about the state of it — better than usual, he said, there's actual visible surface — and I laughed and said I'd found a pen. He said: just the one pen? I said yeah, that was today's project. He went to make dinner and I sat for another minute.

I thought about the train: *I don't know what I'm building toward.* Maybe this was part of the answer, or at least adjacent to it. Not an answer I have to act on tonight. Just something true that I lost track of, that found its way back in through a desk drawer and a pen I recognized, the way things do when you've created the conditions for something quiet to be heard.

The notebook is in the closet. The pen is on the desk.

I don't know what I'm going to do about either of those things.

That's okay for now.

entries.prompt: What's something you've gotten noticeably better at in the last year or two — not because you tried, but because life just kept putting you in the situation until you figured it out? Walk me through how you realized it.
entries.content:
The realization didn't come with any ceremony. It came on a Tuesday in a meeting I'd been in a version of forty times, and afterward I was walking back to my desk and I thought: I don't remember being scared of that.

There's a particular kind of meeting — not the formal presentations, not the stakeholder reviews with the practiced transitions — but the ones where something has gone sideways and several people with competing interests are in a room together and someone has to say the clarifying thing that might make one of them uncomfortable. Those meetings used to take up a lot of space in my body before they happened. I'd go in knowing what needed to be said and spend the first ten minutes managing the fact that I was the one who was going to have to say it, and my voice would do this controlled-neutral thing, flat and careful, which was the voice I used when I was trying not to sound like what I was, which was nervous.

Today one of those meetings happened without warning. No agenda, someone just dropped a calendar invite an hour before with a title vague enough to be ominous, and I went in without any of the usual preparation time. Twenty minutes in, there was a moment where two people were talking past each other in a way that was going to calcify if nobody interrupted, and I interrupted. I said something like: I think what she's describing is actually a different problem from what you're solving for, can we name both of them separately before we try to fix either. And the room kind of — paused, and then both of them said yes, and we named both problems, and the next forty minutes were more useful than they would have been.

Walking back to my desk I was thinking about what to have for lunch and not much else. And then the absence hit me. The absence of the pre-meeting dread, the absence of the careful controlled voice. I'd just done the thing. The interruption, the reframe, the naming of the uncomfortable split — I'd done all of it without it being A Whole Thing first.

I don't know exactly when that changed.

There wasn't a turning point. There was no meeting where I made a breakthrough and felt it solidify into skill. It's more like — I kept being the person who got asked to be in those rooms, and the rooms kept putting me in the same position, and at some point I must have gotten through a hard moment enough times that my nervous system filed it under: survivable, familiar, yours. The knowing moved from my head to somewhere lower and more automatic.

What I've actually gotten better at, I think, is holding two things in the room without flinching. Two people who each believe they're right. Two problems that look like one problem but aren't. Two needs that are going to require someone to shift, and the knowledge that naming that out loud is the only way forward. I used to feel the friction of those moments as something to be managed, absorbed, smoothed over. Now I feel it as information. Where the friction is tells me where the actual problem is. I go toward it rather than around.

I'm not sure when I would have described this to anyone as a thing I was learning. I wouldn't have — that's the thing. I wasn't trying to learn it, I wasn't tracking my own progress, I have no memory of ever saying to Sam or Riley I'm really working on this skill of direct confrontation in group settings. I was just showing up to the meetings because I was asked to, and somewhere in the showing up it happened.

Devon said something to me a few months ago — before the card, before most of the things I've been turning over lately — he said: *you're the person I want in a room when things are about to go wrong.* He said it in a Devon way, light, in passing, and I said something deflecting. Probably attributed it to luck or circumstances, the small-group-size version of a response. But I've thought about it since, and I think he was describing something real.

The Mei thing is interesting here, and I've been sitting with it. I know she values me in those rooms. She's said it in the particular way she says things, which is the observation-at-me rather than direct compliment — she'll say something like *this is exactly the kind of situation where having you in there helps.* Which I then turn over for three days looking for the asterisk. But maybe there isn't an asterisk. Maybe she's just describing what she sees, the same way the woman in the hall described what she saw, the same way Devon described what he saw, and I'm the one who can't take the thing at face value because taking it at face value would mean I'd have to own it.

What I'm better at is the room. What I'm still not good at is letting that be mine.

The writing thing has been in my peripheral vision all week. The pen is still on the desk — I haven't moved it back — and the notebook is presumably still in the closet, and I haven't gone looking for it but I've also been aware of not going looking for it, which is different from forgetting it exists. I keep thinking about Riley's question: and then what. And I keep thinking about the inverse of today's realization — the meeting skill happened because life kept putting me in the room. The writing didn't happen because I stopped going to the room. I put the notebook in the closet and I stopped being the person who got up early Saturday with the thick dark blue cover and the fine-point pen, and whatever you become through showing up, I stopped becoming it.

That's not the same thing as it being gone. I don't know if that's true, but I'm trying it out.

Sam was reading when I got home tonight and I told him about the meeting, not the whole intricate internal version, just the shape of it: something went sideways, I didn't feel scared, I didn't notice until after. He listened and he said: *that's been true for a while, actually. I've watched you go into things like that and come out fine and not even seem to register it.*

I said: why didn't you say something.

He looked at me over his book. He said: *you would have explained why it wasn't really like that.*

I thought about the woman in the hall, the sanding-down, the small-group-size response. I said: okay, fair.

He put the book down. He said: *you're good at that. The rooms where things go wrong. You've been good at it for a while. I think you're the last one to know.*

I didn't deflect. That was the small thing. I looked at him and I said: yeah, I think maybe that's right.

That's about as far as I got. It's not a transformation. But I held it for longer than a block and a half this time, didn't hand it back smaller than it came to me, and that might count for something.

The pen is still on the desk.

entries.prompt: What's something you said out loud recently that surprised you a little — like you didn't quite know you thought that until you heard yourself say it? Walk me through the moment and what came after.
entries.content:
It happened in the middle of a conversation about something else entirely.

Sam and I were talking about the weekend — nothing important, the usual Friday-night negotiating about what we'd do and what we'd let slide. He mentioned that his friend from college, the one who moved up north a couple years ago, was going to be in the city Saturday and wanted to get dinner, a small group, nothing elaborate. He said it the way he always asks me about these things, not pressure in it, just laying it down and waiting.

I felt the usual small tightening. The automatic no, the one that arrives before I've considered anything, the one I've been slowly learning to sit one beat past before I answer.

I sat the beat. And then I said: I think you should go, and I'll probably skip it.

That part was fine, that was new-normal, that was me not walking it back immediately. He nodded. He said he'd tell his friend. No negotiation.

And then I said: I want to stay home and look for the notebook.

We both went quiet for a second.

I don't think I'd said it out loud before.
