I'd thought about the notebook almost every day for a week — the pen on the desk, the thing with some heat in it, the wanting that might be current and not just memorial — but I'd been turning it over in here, in my own handwriting, at a safe distance. I hadn't said it to Sam, I hadn't said it to Riley, I hadn't even said it fully to myself in words, just in the direction I kept not-looking.

And then there it was. A sentence. In the room.

Sam said: the one in the closet?

I said: yeah.

He said: okay. And that was it, the whole conversation, and he went back to his phone and I went back to the dishes I'd been theoretically doing, and I stood at the sink for a minute thinking: did I just say that.

What surprised me wasn't the wanting — I think I'd known about the wanting, the pen on the desk was enough to establish the wanting. What surprised me was how plain the sentence was. *I want to stay home and look for the notebook.* I didn't dress it up, didn't hedge it as maybe I'll poke around, didn't convert it into a productive-sounding plan for the weekend. I just said what I wanted and it came out before I'd managed it.

I kept thinking about it through Friday night. Sam went to sleep before I did — he usually does — and I was on the couch with the lamp on, not doing much. I was thinking about the last time I wanted something privately enough that saying it out loud felt like crossing from one place into another. The mom phone call, maybe. Or buying the ticket. There's a version of wanting things I'm fluent in: the correct-answer wants, the professional wants, the I'd love to own this deck wants. Those I can say clearly, on demand, with my face doing the right thing. The other version, the one with the actual heat in it, I tend to keep in here where no one can ask and then what.

I hadn't planned to say it. It arrived because the room was low-stakes and Sam wasn't asking and I'd already done the small piece of work — sitting the beat, making the easier decision first — and apparently that loosened something.

Saturday morning, Sam left around noon. I heard the door and then the apartment went into its quiet version of itself, the good kind.

I stood in front of the closet for a minute before I opened it. I'm aware of how that sounds. Standing in front of your own closet. But there's something I'd been building the notebook into, over the course of a week of thinking, and I didn't want the thing itself to be a disappointment.

It was in a box under some folded sweaters I don't wear anymore. Dark blue cover, thicker than I remembered, or I remembered it wrong. I carried it to the kitchen table and opened it.

The handwriting surprised me. Mine, obviously, but younger-looking somehow, more forward-leaning, like it was in a hurry. The first few pages were notes to myself, setup, rules of the world I'd been trying to make. Then the scenes. I read for maybe half an hour, just reading, not performing. A couple of things were worse than I remembered. A few things were better, actually better, specific in a way I wasn't expecting — a detail about a room, a sentence a character said that had some real compression in it.

The feeling was strange. Not triumphant, not sad. Something more specific than either. Like running into someone you used to know well and realizing the person you remembered and the actual person are close but not identical, and you're not sure yet if the difference is better or worse, just that it's there and has to be accounted for.

I didn't write anything. I want to say that clearly so I don't let myself make the finding into more than it was. I opened the notebook, I read what was in it, I sat with it for a while at the kitchen table, and then I made coffee and closed it and left it on the table instead of putting it back in the box.

When Sam came home, hours later, he saw it there. He didn't say anything immediately. He made a glass of water, sat down across from me, glanced at it.

He said: how is it?

I said: complicated. Worse in some places than I thought. Better in others.

He said: but worth finding?

I thought about it. The heat-in-the-wanting thing. The sentence that arrived before I managed it. The detail about the room that was actually good, specific, mine.

I said: yeah. I think so.

He nodded. He asked if I'd written anything new and I said no, not yet, I just looked. He said that seemed like the right place to start.

It's on the table still. I keep passing it and not doing anything with it, which is different from not knowing it's there. There's something I'm working up to, or something that's working its way toward me, and I don't know which direction the movement is going but I can feel there is movement.

The show is next Saturday. The notebook is on the table. The pen is on the desk.

I keep saying a version of I don't know what I'm building toward, and I think that's still true, but maybe the inventory is part of it. Seeing what's actually in the room. The pen, the notebook, the forty-five seconds with Devon, the woman in the hall saying the true thing and walking away, Riley's question still open and unanswered. Things I've been given or found or said out loud by accident.

I didn't know I thought that until I heard myself say it. But the saying made it real in some way that the thinking alone hadn't, and now it's out there and Sam knows and the notebook is on the table and I can't unknow any of it.

That seems like how things actually start.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been meaning to tell someone that you've been delivering in smaller, safer pieces instead — the diluted version, not the real thing? Walk me through the last time you handed someone the smaller version and what you held back.
entries.content:
The show was last night. That's not what this is about but I want to say it: I went, I stood in the third row from the stage, I didn't manage anyone's experience, the sound system was exactly what it had any right to be for a room that size, and I came home a little after midnight with my ears ringing in the best way. Sam was still up, which surprised me. He handed me a glass of water and said how was it and I told him while it was still loud in my body, before I'd turned it into anything reportable. He listened. That was last night.

This is about Mei.

There have been a handful of conversations this fall where she's asked me something, or said something, and I've responded in the managed way, the version that's technically true and does not contain the actual thing. I keep doing it. I want to look at the most recent one.

Thursday afternoon — after the meeting I ran, the one without the advance notice, the one where I interrupted the two people talking past each other and named the problem split and it worked. I wrote about that meeting. After it, walking back to my desk. Mei caught me in the hall, which she doesn't always do, and she said: *good instinct in there.* That specific. Not good meeting, not good work, but good instinct, which is a different kind of recognition, more interior, aimed at something I can't entirely prepare for.

I said thank you. So far, fine, no deflection.

And then she said: *I've been thinking about you for some of the work coming up in the next phase. Can we find time next week to talk about it?*

She said it and kept walking, the way she does, like statements don't require immediate response, like she's long past needing to watch the landing. And I said sure, of course, looking forward to it, or some version of that, and she rounded the corner and was gone.

And I stood there for a moment in the hall, and what I felt was — complicated. Genuinely complicated, not in the layered-and-intricate way I often mean that, but in the sense of two things arriving simultaneously and not resolving into each other.

One was the thing it looked like: this is the recognition I've been performing toward for two years. She looked at me and thought: her. That part was real and I let myself feel it for a full beat without diminishing it, which is at least new.

The other thing was the train thought. *I don't know what I'm building toward.* The question with no second step. The one that's been sitting in the room with me all fall, patient and unanswered.

That afternoon I texted Sam: *Mei wants to meet next week, says she's thinking about me for the next phase.* And he wrote back: *that's great, you've been working toward that.* And I wrote: *yeah.* And sent it. And that was the whole conversation.

What I held back: I'm not sure I want it the way I thought I did.

Not that sentence. Not the real shape of what I've been sitting with since the train, since the notebook, since Riley asked and then what and I didn't have an answer. I handed Sam the smaller version — Mei said a good thing, this is good news — and I kept the rest in here, where it's been living with the notebook and the pen and everything else that's been accumulating.

It's not that I don't want the work. I do, I think. The meeting on Thursday — the friction as information, going toward the hard thing rather than around it — that's real, that's mine, I didn't make it up. There's something I'm genuinely good at and I'd like to do more of it. That part of the want is solid.

But underneath: there's also a want that has nothing to do with Mei or the next phase or the structural conversation that's been happening above us. There's a notebook on my kitchen table. There's a sentence a character said that had actual compression in it, that I wrote seven years ago and apparently still had it. There's a want with some heat in it that I named out loud last weekend without planning to, and Sam asked how was it and I said complicated, better in some places than I thought.

I haven't told Mei any of that. Obviously. You don't tell your senior colleague that you found an old novel in the closet and now you're not sure what you're optimizing for. I understand that's not a conversation you have in the hall after a Thursday meeting.

But I also haven't told Sam the full version. Or Riley. I've been putting down small pieces with both of them, the way you pay a bill in installments, and keeping the rest.

The full version is something like: I think I've been performing toward a destination that I chose before I knew much about what I wanted, and now I'm closer to it than I've ever been, and I'm also not sure the destination is where I actually want to go. Both of those things at the same time. Not a crisis, not a resignation, not I'm going to quit and write fiction — nothing that dramatic. Just a genuine uncertainty about whether the shape I've been building myself into is the shape I would have chosen if I'd been paying better attention. And the uncertainty doesn't feel like collapse. It feels like a question that finally has enough air around it to be a question.

I keep almost saying it. I get to the threshold of it with Sam, and then I hand him the smaller piece — the Mei update, the meeting thing, the ticket I bought — and the real thing stays behind. Part of it is what I wrote a few entries ago: if I say the full version, he'll ask what I actually want to do about it. And I don't have that answer. So I give him the reportable version and he responds to the reportable version and we have a real exchange that is not quite the real exchange.

Riley would take it, I think. She'd take the whole complicated version without needing me to resolve it first. She'd say *yeah, that sounds about right, tell me more about the notebook.* She'd ask about the characters again, whether the ones she asked about in our mid-twenties are still in there. She's earned the full version more than anyone, and she's also still in another city and easier to text links to, and I can see myself reaching for that ease again and I don't want to.

I'm meeting with Mei on Wednesday. I'm going to go in and be genuinely present for that conversation, because the work is real and she's been paying attention in a way I've wanted her to pay attention and that deserves to be received properly, not through a layer of ambivalence I haven't finished working through.

And at some point this week I'm going to call Riley and tell her about the train and the notebook and the and then what. Not because I have the answer. Because she asked the question first and she should hear how far I've gotten with it.

The notebook is still on the table. I haven't written in it yet. But I've walked past it a dozen times and not moved it, and yesterday I touched the cover on the way to get my coffee, just briefly, the way you acknowledge something without making it into a moment.

That might be where I am with all of it. Touching the cover. Not putting it back.

entries.prompt: What's something you own that you've been meaning to get rid of for a long time — walk me through the last time you looked at it and decided to leave it where it was.
entries.content:
There's a cardigan in the back of the closet that I looked at again this morning when I was getting dressed. Oatmeal-colored, cable-knit, one of the big shapeless ones from the early part of my twenties when I went through a phase of wanting to disappear into my clothing. I've moved it to four apartments. I've put it in the donation pile twice, both times retrieved it before the bag left the building.

This morning I was standing in the closet in my socks and I pulled it out and held it, which is already more engagement than I usually give it, and I thought: today might be the day.

Then I put it back. Obviously.

I know why I keep it. That's the thing. It's not a mystery I'm using this entry to solve — the why is fairly clear and I just don't like it very much.

The cardigan is from the period when I was working on the notebook. Not the notebook specifically, not in the sense that I wore it to write in, but it's from that era: mid-twenties, the job before this city, the Saturdays getting up early, the characters Riley asked about like they were people she might meet. I wore that cardigan constantly. It was what I wore when I wasn't performing anything. It's big enough to disappear into and that was the point — when you're trying to make something you're not sure you can make, there's something useful about not having a shape.

I haven't worn it in years. It sits in the back of the closet behind the things I actually reach for, and every time I see it I do some version of this: I take it out, I hold it, I put it back. It's become a ritual I'm slightly embarrassed by.

What I'm actually doing, I think, is checking whether I'm still the person who deserves to keep it. Whether the want is still current. Whether putting it in a bag for the donation bin would be a reasonable form of moving on, or whether it would be the same thing as putting the notebook back in the box — the gesture that says I've decided, without having actually decided.

Since I found the notebook two weeks ago I've walked past it every morning. I touched the cover on my way to get coffee and now that's a thing I do, apparently: touch the cover, get the coffee, go to work. The cardigan is in the closet and the notebook is on the table and they're related to each other in a way I don't want to dress up.

Mei and I met on Wednesday. I went in and I was genuinely present, the way I said I would be. She talked about the next phase, what she's thinking about, where she sees the friction points that are going to need someone who can hold several threads and go toward the hard thing rather than around it. She used language that was specific to things I actually do. It was not vague encouragement. It was — accurate, and substantial, and offered without requiring me to perform gratitude. I said yes to the conversation, I didn't say yes to anything specific yet, and she said she didn't need me to, just wanted to see what I thought. We talked for an hour.

I left the room and stood in the hall and felt, in order: pleased, seen, and then almost immediately: so now what.

The and then what. Still no second step. I have been waiting for the recognition to answer the question and instead it just sharpens it.

I called Riley on Wednesday night and told her most of it. The meeting, the and then what, and yes — the notebook on the table, the characters, the compression in the sentence I'd apparently written seven years ago and still had it. She was quiet in the way she is when she's actually listening, and then she said: *do you remember the one character, the one who kept explaining herself to people who hadn't asked?*

I said yeah. I'd forgotten I remembered, but yeah.

She said: *you said she was the most real one.* I said I know. She said: *okay. so.*

She didn't finish the sentence. She's known me since I was twenty-two, she knows she doesn't have to. So means: there you go, there's your answer, are you going to look at it or not.

I've been looking at it.

I think what the cardigan holds is permission. That sounds more significant than I mean it. I mean something smaller and more specific: it's from a time when I had, without deciding to have it, made room in my life for the thing with no second step. The getting-up-early wasn't a system. The notebook wasn't a plan. I wasn't tracking progress or assigning it to a calendar slot or framing it as good for me. I just wanted to make something and I made time to try. The cardigan is the physical evidence that I used to have access to that — whatever it is, that particular kind of permission-giving — and somewhere between then and now I stopped believing it was available.

That's what I'm really holding on to when I take it out of the closet. Not the object. The version of myself who wore it. Checking whether she's still in there or whether I've been wrong to keep something in trust for her this long.

I put it back this morning. But I left the closet door open a few extra seconds, which is probably nothing, but it's something.

The notebook is still on the table. I wrote three sentences in it last night — not in the notebook, on a separate piece of paper, not connected to anything that was already there, just three sentences about a room and a particular quality of light in that room and the way someone stands in a doorway when they're not sure they're welcome. I didn't read them back. I just folded the paper and put it under the notebook, so it's there but I don't have to look at it.

Sam came into the kitchen while I was doing it. He saw the paper, saw me fold it, didn't ask. He started making tea and that was the whole thing, and it was exactly the right amount of being witnessed, somehow — not nothing, not examined. Just: I was doing something and another person was in the room.

The show, the notebook, the Mei meeting, the cardigan. I seem to be in a period of not putting things back. I don't have a theory about where it goes from here. I'm suspicious of the theory-forming part of myself right now anyway — that part has been very busy for years, very productive at explaining why things aren't the right time and the logistics are complicated and better to wait until I know what I actually want.

What I actually want. Here's a version of it, the most honest I can make it tonight:

I want to do the work Mei is describing, and I want to write three sentences on a piece of paper sometimes and fold it under a notebook, and I want both of those things to be real at the same time without needing them to resolve into a coherent story about what I'm building toward. Maybe nothing is actually in conflict. Maybe I've been treating this like a decision about which self to keep, when the answer is closer to: both, apparently, in a big shapeless cardigan if necessary.

I'm not donating it. Not yet.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been putting off that you keep dressing up as not-yet-ready — walk me through the last time you told yourself it wasn't time.
entries.content:
Last Sunday I was sitting at the kitchen table with the notebook open — not writing in it, just open, the way you leave a window cracked when you're not sure about the weather — and I thought: I should write a proper synopsis. Before I go any further. So I know what I'm making.

It's a thing I read once, or heard, about how serious writers work. You have the shape before you have the story. The map before the territory.

So I told myself: not yet. Not until I have the synopsis. And then I closed the notebook and washed out my coffee mug and went to do something else I can't remember.

I've done some version of this at least four times since I found the notebook three weeks ago. The specific requirement changes — sometimes it's the synopsis, sometimes it's that I should re-read everything I've already written before adding to it, sometimes it's that I should find a better notebook, something I'd feel more serious using, because the old one is too freighted now with the version of myself I'm trying to figure out if I'm still continuous with. Each time there's a new condition, a new thing that would need to be true before the actual thing can start. I keep building the runway and not taking off.

I know what this is. I know it in the same way I knew, standing in the card shop holding the Devon card, that the logistics weren't the real problem. I've gotten almost embarrassingly good at identifying the shape of my own avoidance while still fully doing the avoidance. It's like being able to name every ingredient in a dish you're eating without being able to stop eating it.

The true thing, the one under the synopsis requirement and the re-reading requirement and the better-notebook requirement, is something I'd rather not write down because writing it down makes it more available to be disappointed.

Here it is anyway: I'm afraid the three sentences I wrote on the folded piece of paper — the ones about the room and the light and the way someone stands in a doorway when they're not sure they're welcome — I'm afraid those three sentences are the best ones. That I've been sitting on a seven-year-old notebook and I opened it and it had some real compression in it and I managed three more sentences and that's actually all there is. The well that I was drawing from in my mid-twenties, the one that could fill four pages before I ran out of what had started them — I'm afraid I let it sit too long uncovered. Something's gotten in or something's gone out. The cardigan is in the closet but the person who wore it might be gone in a way that's not about the object at all.

*You don't get to find out without trying*, the less patient internal voice says. *The synopsis is not going to tell you anything about the well.* 

I know. I know that. I sat with the notebook open anyway and thought: one more week, once the Mei situation settles, once I know what the next phase is actually going to ask of me, once I have a sense of the shape —

Sam came in for something, opened the fridge, stood there looking at its contents with the expression he makes when he's not actually hungry but wants a reason to be in the room. He glanced at the notebook, back at the fridge, closed it.

He said: "you going to write in it today?"

I said I was thinking about structuring it first.

He said: "ah." The way he says *ah* when he wants me to hear how it sounds without him having to editorialize.

I said: I just don't want to go in the wrong direction.

He got a glass of water he didn't seem to need and leaned against the counter. "What would the wrong direction be?"

I didn't have an answer that held up. I gave him something about narrative structure, about knowing your ending before you begin, about wanting to be intentional. He listened with his patient face and then he said: "or you could just write something and see what it wants to be."

Which is the simplest possible thing and I've known it the whole time. The notebook didn't care about my intentions in my mid-twenties. The sentences didn't come because I had a plan. They came because I showed up to the page before I'd convinced myself I wasn't ready.

The Mei meeting is part of it too, though I've been reluctant to say that even here.

She's offering me the kind of work I've performed toward for two years and now it's in front of me and I keep noticing that the notebook is on the table. The two things keep being in the same room, and I've been treating that as a contradiction — like if the next phase at work is substantive and real, there's no available space for the folded piece of paper, for whatever the three sentences were the beginning of, for the getting-up-early if I can find my way back to it. Like adulthood has a limited number of serious slots and they don't coexist.

I haven't worked out whether that's true yet. But I've been using the not-having-worked-it-out as a reason to hold both things at a distance. Everything on pause until I understand the terms. Nothing started until I know what I'm building.

Riley would not accept this framing. I can hear her already: *you are describing a philosophy of life in which you wait until you're sure before you begin anything, and then you wonder why nothing begins.* She'd say it gently. She'd also be right.

I told her some of this on the phone earlier this week. The synopsis thing, the re-reading thing, the better-notebook thing. She listened and then she said: "are you waiting until you're good enough or until you're not afraid?"

I said I didn't know the difference.

She said: "I think you do."

The conversation moved on. We talked about her thing, her city, a person she's been cautiously not-calling-a-relationship. I listened and asked follow-up questions and it was the real kind of conversation, the ones we do now sometimes, the forty-minutes-without-noticing. Before we hung up she said: *write in the notebook. Not tomorrow. Tonight.* She said it the way she says things that are true, slightly slower, picking it up and looking at it.

I said I'd think about it.

I have been thinking about it since.

The table, tonight: the open notebook, the pen, the folded paper underneath with the three sentences on it. I keep coming to the table and finding reasons to be there for other purposes — coffee, laptop, the general gravity of the kitchen. I sit near the notebook and do adjacent things. I'm good at adjacent things.

I understand the synopsis isn't the point. I understand the better notebook isn't the point. I understand the point is to find out if the well is still there, and the only test for that is to lower the bucket.

I don't know if this is the night. I keep thinking that sentence, too, and recognizing it as another version of not yet.

The notebook is open. The pen is there.

At some point the next thing I do won't be writing about writing. It'll just be writing. I'm trying to locate where that starts — the exact moment when the preparing ends and the thing itself begins, the not-ready shading into ready without announcement, the way the October light doesn't arrive, it's just suddenly the afternoon and there it is.

I think it might be close.

entries.prompt: What's something you've changed your mind about in the last year — not dramatically, but quietly, without announcing it to anyone? Walk me through when you realized the shift had already happened.
entries.content:
I wrote in the notebook last night.

Not the synopsis. Not a re-reading of the old pages, not a careful structural decision about where to begin. I just picked up the pen and wrote until I ran out, which took about forty minutes and produced something I'm not going to read back for a few days. The not-reading-back-immediately is itself new, or maybe old — I think I used to do that, trust the thing to exist on its own without checking whether it was good yet. Either way, it happened. The notebook is no longer just an object I touch on the way to coffee. That's settled.

But that's not actually what I've been thinking about today.

---

I've changed my mind about what Mei's opinion of me is for.

I didn't announce it. There was no conversation with myself where I said: this is the day the thing shifts. It shifted over the course of the fall, I think, slowly enough that I kept not noticing, and then last Wednesday I was walking back to my desk after a meeting and I realized I'd been in the room with Mei for an hour and I hadn't once checked her face for information about how I was doing. I'd been in the conversation. Just in it. And the hour had been good and I'd said real things and she'd responded to the real things and at no point had I felt the specific vigilance — that slight bracing, the monitoring, the constant peripheral awareness of her expression — that I've had in most rooms with her for two years.

I noticed the absence the way you notice you've stopped hearing a sound. Not when it stops — after.

The monitoring started early. I think it was with me before Mei, calibrated to other faces, the way I wrote in an earlier entry — the professor, the first-job someone, the faces that update while the structure stays. But Mei became the primary version, and I built something around it: the whole project of saying yes before my brain had any say, of performing toward her idea of exceptional, of treating her good opinion like a thing I had to keep paying for or it would expire. I've written about this enough times that it started to feel like just describing a fixed fact about myself, the way you'd describe your height or your handwriting. That's the shape of this thing and this is what it does.

Except it's not quite the same shape anymore.

I think what changed isn't how much I want her to think well of me. I still do. That's not gone. What changed is that I stopped experiencing it as load-bearing. It used to feel like — if she looked at me one day and revised her opinion downward, something structural would fail. I couldn't have told you exactly what, but the failure felt material, real, consequential in some way I'd never managed to make concrete. And I arranged a lot of behavior around preventing it, including the arrangements I could see (the deck, the yes-before-thinking) and the ones I couldn't quite see until I was sitting in my kitchen alone on a Saturday with very correct posture.

Somewhere this fall, I think the belief that she was load-bearing quietly stopped being true, and I didn't notice because the behavior was still running.

What replaced it is something I still don't have great language for. Something closer to: I want her to think well of me because I think well of her, and that's a different kind of want. There's an asymmetry in the original version — I need something from her and she's unaware of holding it. This version is more mutual in some way. She's been paying attention, accurately, to things I actually do. I can take that seriously without making it structural. I can let it matter without letting it be the floor.

The meeting a few weeks ago, where she said good instinct and told me she'd been thinking about me for the next phase — what I felt was pleased, and then: so now what. The so now what is still there, unanswered in some ways. But I've stopped experiencing the pleasure and the uncertainty as contradictions. They're both just true, simultaneously, about the same moment. Something I've apparently learned to tolerate this fall, the holding of more than one thing without one of them having to win.

I told some of this to Sam on the walk we took this afternoon. Not in the layered version, more like the shape of it: I think I've been treating Mei's opinion as more structurally important than it actually is, and I think it's getting less so, and I'm not sure when that started but I noticed it this week. He was quiet for a minute. We were at the part of the walk where the path runs alongside the water and the wind was up, doing things to the surface. He said: "do you know why it shifted?"

I said I'd been thinking about that. My best guess, I told him, is that I found some other things this fall. The notebook. The meeting I ran without the pre-meeting dread. The show, the forty-five seconds with Devon, the cardigan in the closet I keep deciding to keep. Things that have some weight on their own, that don't derive their weight from what Mei thinks about them. When you have enough of those, maybe the ones that are running on borrowed weight start to feel less necessary. Maybe you stop needing the floor to be somewhere outside yourself because enough floor has accumulated under your own feet.

He said: "that's a good way to put it." Which from Sam means he's thought about this before and was waiting for me to get here.

I didn't ask how long he'd been waiting. I could guess, and the guess would probably be humbling.

The thing about quiet shifts is that they're hard to trust at first. I've written in this journal about wanting Mei's good opinion in a way I described as outsized, and I've analyzed it from several angles, and I've noticed it running even when I didn't want it to, and none of the noticing moved it much. Then the fall happened — the walk before work, the fifteen quiet minutes, the phone calls with Riley, the notebook coming out of the closet — and something moved that I wasn't tracking, through means I can't fully account for. That's unsatisfying if you want a method. But it might be true.

The other thing I've quietly changed my mind about: I used to think wanting the work-thing and wanting the notebook-thing were in tension, that I'd have to figure out which one to take seriously before I could take either seriously. I stopped believing that somewhere around the cardigan. I couldn't tell you the exact day.

Both things are on the table. Literally, the notebook is on the table. The Mei conversation is also real, the next phase is coming, the work that uses the skills I've actually built is going to be there in January whether I've decided what I'm building toward or not. I don't have to resolve the question before I can show up to both.

I didn't announce any of this. It just accumulated until it was true, and then I walked alongside the water with Sam and said it out loud, and he said that's a good way to put it, and we kept walking.

The notebook has writing in it now. Not the three sentences — new ones, more, pages of new ones. The well is still there.

I haven't told Mei. She doesn't need to know. It was never really about her.

entries.prompt: What's something you did recently that you were sure would feel worse than it did — walk me through the moment you realized you were going to be okay.
entries.content:
I have been bracing for the Mei meeting since she put it on the calendar three weeks ago. Not the Wednesday meeting from before — this was the follow-up, the one where we'd actually talk about what the next phase asks of me and whether I want to be asked. We'd been circling the substance for weeks. Last Thursday was the substance.

I walked in there with the particular kind of readiness that feels a lot like dread dressed in better clothes. Prepared, in the way I prepare for things — transitions practiced not quite to the point of not hearing the words, but close. I had thought about what she might say. I had thought about what I'd say back. I had also, underneath all of that, been holding the specific fear that the meeting itself would clarify something I've been sitting with all fall: that the thing I've been performing toward would, once offered, feel hollow. That getting the thing would be its own kind of problem.

She was straightforward in the way she's always straightforward. The next phase has a specific character, harder than what I've been doing, requires someone who can hold ambiguity without converting it to false clarity too quickly. She said false clarity — I wrote it down after, on the train home, because it was specific enough that it named something I'd been trying to name. She said: *the work will be harder to assess, you won't have the clean feedback loops you're used to.* She said she'd been watching me in the rooms where things go sideways and she thinks I've got something she can't teach.

I sat across from her and I received it. Actually received it, not at a remove, not through the layer of checking her expression for asterisks.

Then she asked: *do you want it?*

Just that. Direct and waiting.

I thought, for a split second, about the correct answer. The one that's been ready for two years, the I'd love to, the yeah absolutely, the warm and willing face already doing its thing. I could feel the shape of it, the muscle memory of it. And then I thought about the train, the tunnel wall, the thought that arrived without any preparation: *I don't know what I'm building toward.* I thought about Sam asking do you want to go to the show and me saying yes and him saying then buy the ticket. I thought about the notebook, the pages I wrote last week, the well that's still there.

I said: yes, I think so. I said: I want to be honest with you, I've been doing some thinking this fall about what I actually want from work, as opposed to the answer I give when someone asks, and the honest version is that I think I do want this, I'm also still working out what everything else is for. I said it approximately like that, not as cleanly — there was some fumbling, a restated sentence, a place where I paused longer than felt comfortable. But the substance was there.

She looked at me for a moment. I waited for the flattening, the slight recalibration, the look that says: I asked a professional question and you gave me a personal one.

She said: *good. I don't want someone who's on autopilot about it.*

That was it. She moved on to logistics, the actual details, what January looks like, who else is involved. I took notes. The meeting ended. She said she was glad we'd talked and she seemed to mean it in the ordinary way, not layered with anything.

I stood in the hall afterward and waited.

I was waiting for the hollow feeling. The so-now-what emptiness I'd been half-expecting, the evidence that getting the thing doesn't answer the question. And there was some of that — I'm not going to pretend the question dissolved. I still don't know what I'm building toward in the largest sense. The notebook is still on the table and I've written in it four times now and I haven't resolved the thing about what it means alongside everything else.

But the hollow feeling didn't have the weight I'd given it in advance. It was just — a smaller uncertainty, sitting next to a larger okay. The okay was the bigger thing.

I walked to the window at the end of the hall, the one that looks out over the street, and I stood there for a minute. The city was doing its late-fall version of itself, grey and particular, people moving fast on the sidewalk below because it was cold. I thought: you said the true thing and she didn't need you to unsay it. The floor held.

I texted Sam from the window: *it went okay. more than okay. tell you tonight.* He sent back a single punctuation mark, the one that means: good, I'm here, take your time.

Devon walked by with his coffee and said something easy and I laughed at the easy thing and that was the normal fabric of the afternoon reasserting itself, which I was glad for. The extraordinary moment becoming ordinary again in the way everything does, which used to feel like loss and lately feels more like relief.

On the train home I wrote down the false clarity phrase and then I sat with the window and the familiar tunnel dark and thought about the moment she asked if I wanted it and I didn't give the automatic answer. Something I've been training out of myself for months without exactly knowing I was training, through a series of smaller moments — the card at lunch, the ticket before coffee, the phone to my ear with Riley, the sentence that arrived before I managed it, *I want to stay home and look for the notebook.* All those small non-automatics, accumulated.

I didn't know they'd add up to anything. That's the part I couldn't have predicted. You make the smaller choice — you sit the beat before answering, you buy the ticket before you've reasoned yourself out of it, you say the fumbling true thing instead of the clean practiced one — and somewhere in the accumulation of smaller choices, something changes that you weren't tracking.

Sam was home when I got there. He'd made dinner, which he doesn't always, and I told him the shape of the meeting over the food: what she said, what I said, the specific phrase, the waiting for hollow that didn't fully arrive. He listened. He said: "did it feel like you, in there?"

I thought about it. The fumbling, the restated sentence, the pause that lasted a beat too long. The absence of the managed voice.

I said: yeah, actually. More than usual.

He said that was probably the thing.

We did the dishes after. He washed, I dried. I was in it for once, not somewhere else in my head, just standing at the counter with a dish towel and the sound of water and the ordinary warm Wednesday night. The bowl I dried I dried once and put away.

I don't know that I was sure it would feel worse. I knew I was afraid it would. But the afraid had so much space in my chest that it had started to feel like certainty, and certainty about how bad something will be is its own kind of distance — you suffer the thing before it happens and then when it happens it can't quite reach you through all the pre-suffering.

What I'm trying to remember is the moment in the hall afterward. The waiting that wasn't answered by catastrophe. The grey city below the window and the floor holding and the ordinary afternoon resuming.

The notebook is on the table. I'm going to write in it before I sleep.

entries.prompt: What's something you said recently that you meant less than the other person heard — walk me through the conversation and what you let stand uncorrected.
entries.content:
Last week someone asked me how I was doing with everything and I said fine, really good actually, and they took it completely at face value and moved on, and I let them, and I've been thinking about it since.

It was Devon. We were getting coffee in the kitchen, the midmorning version where it's not actually about the coffee, just the standing somewhere else for five minutes. He asked how I was feeling about the next phase, now that Mei and I had talked. Friendly, easy, Devon-shaped question, not looking for anything in particular.

I said: honestly, really good. I feel like it's the right fit, I feel like I've been working toward something like this and it's landing the way it should.

He said that's great, he meant it, he gave me the Devon version of warmth which is slightly deflating but genuine, and he went back to his desk.

And the thing is — that wasn't a lie. That's the complicated part. The meeting with Mei was more than okay, the floor held, I said the fumbling true thing and she didn't need me to unsay it. All of that is real. The rightness of the fit is real. I didn't invent what I told Devon.

But there's a part I didn't add. The part that's been sitting underneath the okay-and-then-some, quiet and insistent, since Thursday. Which is: I don't entirely know what I said yes to. Not in the practical sense — Mei was specific, January has a shape, I understand what the work involves. I mean something underneath the practical. I said yes to the thing she was offering and I also said something out loud to her that I've never said in that building before, about still working out what everything is for. And she received it without needing me to take it back. And the meeting ended. And I went home and Sam made dinner and I dried the bowl once and put it away and went to bed.

And now I have the next-phase thing, and I also still have the question. The question didn't leave when I took the meeting. I think I expected it to. I think somewhere underneath all the thinking-through I'd been doing this fall, I'd built in an assumption that the resolution of the external situation would do something to the internal one. That getting the offer would either answer the and-then-what or make it stop mattering. And instead what happened is: I got the offer and the question is exactly where I left it, patient, looking at me from across the room.

I didn't say that part to Devon. He didn't ask for that part — it wasn't that kind of conversation, it's never that kind of conversation with Devon, which is part of why I like him, and part of why I sometimes use the easiness of him to avoid saying anything that requires more. I gave him the true-but-incomplete version and watched it land as simply true, and I let it stand.

The letting-stand is the part I'm turning over.

There have been a lot of small moments this fall where I've pushed through the reflex, said the fumbling thing, didn't hand back the compliment smaller than it came to me. And this one I didn't. I had the opening — how are you feeling about it — and I gave the surface version, and Devon accepted it, and now he thinks I'm navigating the transition smoothly and maybe I am, outwardly, but I'm also sitting here two days later trying to figure out what I actually signed up for.

The thing I let stand uncorrected is the impression that I'm resolved. That the fall happened, and I did the work of it, and now I've landed on the other side in a place that makes sense. And I do feel more like myself than I did in September, that's real too. The notebook has writing in it. The monitoring in the Mei meeting was quieter than it's ever been. I said the true thing when she asked if I wanted it. These things happened and I'm not undoing them.

But resolved isn't the word. I'm not sure resolved is ever going to be the word, and I think what I gave Devon was the version of me that suggests otherwise.

I might have said the same to Sam if he'd asked me in a certain way, in a certain moment. But Sam has the longer context, he's been in the room for all of it, he asks the follow-up question, the *how do you mean* or the *what would the wrong direction be.* He makes it harder to stop at the surface version, not because he pushes but because he waits with such particular patience that the air between the surface thing and the real thing becomes impossible to ignore. Devon doesn't have that. Devon takes me at face value, which is mostly a relief, except in moments like this one where the face-value version lets me off a hook I maybe shouldn't be off.

I don't think I owed Devon more than I gave him. I want to say that. It was a kitchen conversation about coffee. The level of disclosure I'm describing isn't something you hand to a coworker on a Tuesday morning no matter how easy they are to be around.

But I owe myself the recognition that I did it. That I had the opening and I chose the smooth version, and Devon walked away thinking I've got it figured out, and I have some of it figured out, and there's a remainder I haven't named out loud to anyone except in fragments. The notebook. Sam in careful pieces. Riley in the real-conversation way, but even then, even with Riley — I've been giving her the version that's moving forward, not the version that's also still uncertain.

Maybe that's just the truth of it. Moving forward and uncertain at the same time, and the version I give people emphasizes the moving because that's the part that's comfortable to share.

The question I keep coming back to is whether the letting-stand is avoidance or just — appropriate editing. Whether there's a version of this I should be saying out loud to someone, or whether the saying is actually what I'm doing here, in the kitchen at the table at night, and that's the right place for it.

I don't think I'm going to go find Devon and amend my answer. That would be its own performance, the conspicuously self-aware version of honesty. I'll let it stand with him.

But I might call Riley this week and say: I took the meeting, and it went well, and I also don't entirely know what I said yes to, and the and-then-what is still there, unchanged, watching me from across the room.

She'll say: *and then what.*

I'll say: I don't know yet.

She'll say: *okay. Keep going.*

That seems about right.

entries.prompt: What's something you did this week mostly for someone else's comfort — walk me through the moment you made that call and what you set aside to do it.
entries.content:
Tuesday there was a meeting I asked for. I initiated it, put it on the calendar myself, which is not nothing — I don't usually do that, I usually wait to be convened. It was with a person from another team who's going to be involved in the next phase, someone I'll be coordinating with closely starting in January. I wanted to establish something before we were in the middle of it. Not strategy, exactly. Just — contact. A sense of each other.

The meeting was fine. She was direct, easy to talk to, the kind of person who doesn't need ten minutes of preamble to get to the point. We figured out some of the logistics, talked about where the friction points would likely be, made a loose plan for how we'd handle the handoffs. I came out of it feeling like January had more shape.

None of that is what I'm thinking about.

About twenty minutes in, she mentioned a way of structuring the reporting that she'd used before, somewhere else, and asked what I thought. It was a reasonable approach. It also wasn't the one I'd been thinking about — I'd been turning over a different structure, less tidy on the surface, but I thought it tracked the actual complexity better, the false clarity thing, the thing Mei had said and I'd written down on the train. I'd had it in my head going into the meeting. I thought it was right.

She looked at me and waited.

And I said: yeah, that makes sense, let's do it that way.

I didn't hesitate very long. A beat, maybe two, and then the easy agreement, and her face smoothed into the settled look of someone whose suggestion has been accepted, and the conversation moved forward.

I drove home from work that day turning it over, trying to understand what had actually happened in those two beats. Because she wasn't Mei. There was nothing at stake the way there's something at stake with Mei, no years of performing-toward, no installed version of her behind my sternum running its assessments. I'd met her exactly once before. The stakes were low in every external sense.

But she'd made a suggestion, and she'd had an expression while she was making it, and the expression was the particular one where someone has already committed a little to the idea, and I felt the moment where pushing back would cost her something — not much, just the small thing it costs you when you've put forward a way of doing things and someone says actually, I was thinking differently. The small deflation of that. And I didn't want to cause it. First meeting, new relationship, January still months away, why start with friction when agreement is right there, so easy, just say yes and the moment passes clean.

The thing I set aside: I still think my structure is better. I thought it then and I think it now.

I keep trying to decide whether Tuesday was a reasonable judgment call — sometimes you defer on something small to build goodwill, that's not purely self-abnegating, that's strategy — or whether it was the old thing, the automatic smoothing, the can't-stand-to-cause-the-small-deflation thing I've been slowly trying to learn to tolerate. I've been going back and forth on it for two days without landing anywhere satisfying.

What I know is that I made the decision in about two seconds without thinking through which kind of moment it was. The reflex fired and I didn't sit the beat. That's the part I keep coming back to, not the outcome, not even the specific disagreement — just that I didn't stop to ask myself if I meant it before I said it. I was back in the familiar motion before I knew I was moving.

I told Sam last night. Not the whole analysis, just the shape of it: I had a thought going into a meeting, someone else suggested something different, I went along with it and I'm not sure I should have.

He asked if I could bring it back up with her.

I said I probably could. We don't have anything locked in yet. I could send her a note saying I've been thinking more about the structure and I want to float another approach. She's the kind of person who'd take that fine. The first meeting wasn't a contract.

He said: so why haven't you.

I didn't have a clean answer. Something about not wanting to look inconsistent, not wanting to open the new relationship with a reversal, not being entirely sure my way is right enough to be worth the friction.

He said: are those all the same reason.

I sat with that for a moment. They're not all the same reason. The not-sure-I'm-right-enough is at least honest engagement with the question. The rest of it is the same old thing — the other person's comfort, the preservation of the smooth surface, the belief that friction costs more than it does.

I said: probably not, no.

He went back to his book. Not dismissively — he does that sometimes, says the useful thing and then gives me the room, because he knows I don't need him to stay in it with me.

I'm going to send the note. Not tonight, but tomorrow, before I've had long enough to talk myself out of it. Something short, nothing that over-explains — I've been thinking more about how we structure the reporting and I want to share a different approach, can we take twenty minutes this week. She'll either like it or she won't, and either way the next phase will have started with me saying the thing I actually thought rather than the smooth agreeable version.

The harder question, the one I'm not fully done with: how often do I not notice when I do this. Tuesday I noticed because the thing I'd set aside was concrete, sitting right there in my own head, recent enough to still have edges. But the automatic smoothing happens at speed, and sometimes the thought I didn't follow hasn't left enough of a trace to examine afterward. How many Tuesdays have there been this year where the reflex fired and I didn't even register the two beats.

That's not a question I can answer tonight. It's probably not a question with a clean answer at all — you can't retroactively recover what the reflex erased. You can only try to sit the beat more often going forward, which I've been trying, inconsistently, with inconsistent results, which might be the best available description of how this kind of change actually goes.

The notebook is on the table. I wrote in it for a while this afternoon, a scene I hadn't planned, just what came. The character who keeps explaining herself to people who haven't asked — she's still in there, I haven't written my way out of her yet, but something shifted in how she was moving through a particular room and I followed it and didn't stop to check if it was the right direction.

I think that's the practice. For the notebook, for Tuesday, for all of it. Follow the thought before the reflex fires. Notice the two beats. Choose rather than smooth.

Tomorrow, the note. Short, specific, mine.

entries.prompt: What's something a friend or family member said to you recently — not during a hard conversation, just offhand — that you've found yourself turning over since? Walk me through what they said and why it keeps coming back.
entries.content:
I sent the note about the reporting structure on Wednesday morning. She wrote back within the hour, said let's look at it, and we took twenty minutes on Thursday and she liked the approach — not immediately, there was a moment in the middle where she pushed back and I held the position, and then something shifted, and she said okay, I see what you're doing. That's settled. I mention it only to say: it went fine. The friction cost less than I'd protected against.

This isn't about that either.

Mom called on Sunday, the weekly-ish call, and we did the usual loop — how's work, how's Sam, have I been sleeping. I've gotten better at receiving the slightly pointed questions as love with imprecise aim, the way I said before, and most of the call was easy in the way those calls are when nothing is wrong and we've run out of news and we're just — present with each other, in the limited way you can be over a phone call on a Sunday afternoon.

Near the end, she was telling me about something small that happened with a neighbor, a mild piece of neighborhood drama she was characterizing with the particular dryness she only uses when she's found something genuinely absurd. I was laughing, real laughing, and she was laughing, and then she said — still in that light register, not building to anything: *you sound like yourself lately.*

And then she said she had to start dinner, and we said we love you, and that was the call.

I've been turning it over since. I can't stop.

You sound like yourself lately. Seven words, offhand, already in the past tense in her mind before she'd finished saying them. She wasn't making a point. She wasn't opening a conversation about how I'd seemed before, or asking me to confirm something, or offering the observation as a question I was supposed to answer. She just said it the way you say true things when they occur to you, lightly, and moved on.

What I keep getting stuck on is the *lately*. The implicit before it contains. Because she's been talking to me every week, she has the longitudinal data in a way most people don't, and she noticed something — not a transformation, nothing she'd frame that way, just that something had returned to calibration. And I hadn't said anything to her about the fall. Not the train thought, not the notebook, not the Mei meetings, not the show, not the card for Devon, not any of it. She was working from the voice alone. Just the weekly voice, over weeks, and something in it had apparently shifted enough that she named it without meaning to make a thing of it.

I don't know if I should be glad or unsettled. I think I'm a little of both and that's probably right.

The glad part is obvious. Something real happened this fall — I've been writing about it in pieces without being able to see the whole shape — and here is evidence from outside that the real thing is detectable. That it's not just me, in the kitchen, turning the same thoughts over until they've worn smooth. Someone who wasn't watching for it noticed anyway. The person who asks slightly pointed questions about whether I'm sleeping enough, who I have kept at the same careful pleasant distance I've kept most people this year, heard something in my voice that I haven't said out loud to her and called it by its right name.

The unsettled part is harder to sit with. If I sound like myself *lately*, then there's a period — a stretch, probably long — when I didn't. And I was functional through all of it. Well-liked, well-employed, doing the deck, saying yeah absolutely, taking my walk before work, checking my email before I'd been awake for ten minutes. From the outside I looked like someone who had it basically together. I probably looked that way to my mother too, on the weekly calls. And she still noticed. The voice gave something away that I'd been carefully not giving away, and she'd been registering it without comment for however long it had been happening, and now it's over, apparently, and she noticed that too.

The gap between exterior and interior is not new information about myself. I've written it here in different ways, the interior louder than the exterior, the professional sentence and the eight-year-old feeling running in parallel. But there's something about having it confirmed from outside — by my mother, offhandedly, on a Sunday — that makes it more concrete than any of my own noting of it ever has. Like seeing a photograph of something you've only ever looked at directly.

I've been thinking about calling her back. Not to have the big conversation — I'm not sure there is one, or that she'd want it, or that she's been sitting with this the way I have. She said the thing and she moved on. That's her. She notices and she says it simply and she doesn't make you answer for it, which is the version of her I find easiest, the one without the slightly pointed questions, the one that's just — accurate and then gone.

I might not call. It might be enough to carry it, the way she carried what she'd been noticing. Some things don't need to be discussed to be real. That's something I've been learning this fall in the context of the notebook, the pen on the desk, the three sentences on the folded paper — that doing a thing quietly doesn't make it less done. You don't have to announce what's happening for it to be happening. The well was still there even when I wasn't looking for it.

Sam was in the other room when my mom called. Afterward I went and sat next to him on the couch and I said: my mom said I sound like myself.

He looked up from his book. He didn't ask what I meant, he just looked at me for a moment with the patient face, the one that means he's listening.

I said: I think she's right, actually.

He said: I know.

I said: how long have you known.

He thought about it. He said: a while. He said: I wasn't sure you were ready to hear it.

I sat with that. I wasn't sure he was wrong about the timing. I think if he'd said it earlier I'd have done the thing where I found the way it wasn't quite right, the asterisk, the complication that required it to be less true than stated. I would have explained why it was more complicated than that. And it is more complicated than that — I still don't know what I'm building toward, the and-then-what is still open, I let the reflex fire on Tuesday before I caught it, there's no version of this where I'm done with any of it.

But underneath the complicated version there's also a simpler one, and my mother found it with seven words on a Sunday, and I think that's the one I want to let stand for now.

I sound like myself.

I want to remember how long it took to get here, not so I can feel bad about the time, just so I know it's real. Things that take a long time to shift are probably actually shifted when they shift. It's not the kind of change you can walk back in a moment of bad faith.

The notebook is on the table.

I'm going to write in it before the week starts.

entries.prompt: What's something you did recently that you later realized you'd been rehearsing for longer than you knew — not practicing out loud, but carrying around, turning over, until the moment arrived and you just… did it?
entries.content:
The Wednesday meeting with her — the one about the reporting structure — I keep coming back to it, but not the way I've been thinking about it. Not the note I sent, not the friction that cost less than I'd protected against. Something smaller that happened inside the meeting.

She'd pushed back on my approach, the moment I mentioned before. And I held the position.

That's the part. I held it.

I said something like: I hear you, and I think the structure you're describing is cleaner on the surface, but I want to stay with this one because I think the surface-clean version flattens something we're going to need to see. She looked at me and I looked back and there was maybe four seconds where nothing happened and then she said: okay, show me.

And I showed her, and then she said I see what you're doing, and that was that.

I've been thinking about those four seconds. How I didn't fill them. How I didn't immediately start softening the thing I'd said, finding the ways she might be right too, building her a ladder back to her original idea so no one had to feel the friction of the disagreement. I just waited. And the waiting didn't feel like bravery, that's what I keep noticing. It felt like something I'd done before. Something whose territory I already knew.

I tried to figure out when I'd learned it and I couldn't find a clear origin. There was no meeting where I practiced the four seconds. No conversation where I said to myself: this time I'm going to hold. I've been bad at this, actually, for most of my working life — I've written enough in here about the automatic smoothing, the yes-before-thinking, the two beats I don't stop to sit with. The reflex firing before I've even registered there was a choice.

But somewhere I must have done this enough times in small enough doses that my nervous system filed it the way it's filed the difficult rooms: survivable, familiar, yours. I just didn't notice I was accumulating it.

I was walking to work this morning — the before-work walk, the one I keep not putting on the calendar because it stops working as soon as it becomes an obligation — and I was trying to trace it back. Where does the four-seconds-of-not-filling come from. And what surfaced, a little unexpectedly, was Riley.

Not a specific conversation. The texture of all of them. The way she'd say something true and then be quiet, genuinely quiet, not building more argument on top of it, not filling the space with reassurance that I didn't have to accept the true thing. She's been doing that since we were twenty-two. Saying the accurate thing and waiting. *You've always done this thing where you decide in advance what the worst version of other people's opinions is.* *Does knowing help.* *You sound like you're waiting until you're not afraid.* All of it offered and then left there. She never chased me into it. She let the thing sit in the air between us and trusted that I could decide whether to pick it up.

I've been on the receiving end of that for almost a decade and I think I've been slowly learning the shape of it without knowing that's what I was learning. The way you absorb someone's cadence without meaning to. You spend enough time with a person who doesn't fill the silence and eventually you start to carry some of their silence with you.

The four seconds in that meeting. They came from somewhere. I think they came from Riley.

There's another one, and it's smaller and I almost didn't write it down.

Last night Sam was telling me about something from his day, a friction with a person at his work, the kind of thing that's annoying and will probably resolve. I was actually listening — not the parallel-tracks version, not managing my own interior while the exterior responds correctly. Just listening. He said something and I asked a follow-up question and he looked slightly surprised, the way you look when someone's question lands closer than expected. He said: yeah, actually, that's exactly the thing.

And I thought: I don't always do that. I've been better at it lately but there have been years of dinners, years of him telling me something and me listening with half of myself while the other half was running its loop about Mei or the deck or what the correct answer to some professional question was going to be. He never complained about it. He's not a complainer. But I know the difference between how I was then and how I was last night and I don't think it's a small difference.

I didn't teach myself to listen by deciding to listen better. I think I just — gradually had less running in the other room. The Mei monitoring quieted. The professional-grading interior got less constant. And the space that opened up went somewhere, and some of it went into actually being in the room with my partner while he told me about his day.

I couldn't have planned that. There's no intervention that would have produced it directly. The path was entirely indirect: find the notebook, send the note, call Riley, buy the ticket before making coffee, say the fumbling true thing to Mei about still working out what everything is for. None of those things were practice for listening to Sam. And somehow they all were.

I said something to Sam about this after, clumsily — I said: I feel like I've been more here this fall. He said: yeah. Just that. Then he said: it's good. And went back to his book.

The things I've been rehearsing without knowing I was rehearsing: not filling the silence. Saying the real thing when the moment arrives. Being in the room I'm actually in. The notebook, the four seconds, the before-work walk I take because it helps and not because it's on a calendar. None of them were practiced. All of them were somehow accumulated.

I don't want to make this into a theory. The theory-forming part of me is ready with a whole architecture about how change works, and I'm suspicious of it because that part has been wrong about a lot of things this year. What I know is smaller: I held a position for four seconds and it felt like somewhere I'd been before. And I can't find the rehearsal, only the result.

Maybe that's what it looks like when something actually takes. Not a decision, not a practice, not a commitment you write down and track. Just a morning when you're standing across from someone and the silence opens up and you don't rush to close it, and later you think: where did that come from, and the only honest answer is: I have no idea, but here it is.

The notebook's on the table. Walk in the morning was good.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been looking forward to that you haven't told anyone about yet — walk me through what it is and what's kept you from saying it out loud.
entries.content:
There's a specific afternoon in the notebook — not in the old pages, in the new ones, the ones from the last month — where the character who keeps explaining herself is in a room she's never been in before, and she's not explaining anything. She's just standing there, looking out a window, and the window is in a city she doesn't know the name of yet, and I haven't decided what she sees. I've been looking forward to finding out.

That's the thing I haven't said out loud. Not the novel as a concept, not the fact of writing in the notebook — Sam knows about that, Riley knows, the notebook is on the table, that part is no longer a secret I'm keeping. I mean this specific thing: that I'm looking forward to what happens next in it. That there's a character in a room and she's waiting for me to figure out what she sees through that window and I'm actually — eager. A little impatient, even. The way you feel the day before a trip, when the packing is done and there's nothing to do but wait for morning.

I don't know when that started. Somewhere between the first night I wrote in it — the forty minutes, the not-reading-back — and now. The early sessions were more effortful, more laden with whether-this-is-any-good, more aware of themselves as attempts. This week something shifted. I was at the table on Tuesday evening while Sam was reading, and I wrote for an hour and when I stopped it wasn't because I'd run out or because I was checking whether it was working. It was just late and I was tired and I wanted to go to sleep and come back to it, the way you set a book down not because you've lost interest but because you want it to still be there in the morning.

That feeling — I'd forgotten what it felt like. It's what the before-work walk feels like when it's working, the sense that something will still be there, that the good thing is not spent by being visited once. I've been sitting with it all week and I haven't told anyone because I don't entirely trust it yet.

That's the honest reason. The not-saying is protective, and I know that, and I'm trying to look at it clearly without talking myself out of the protection too fast.

The thing I'm afraid of, the specific version: I'll say it out loud and it will become an object. The way the ticket became an object — the thing I was either going to accomplish or fail to accomplish — except with something that matters more than a ticket. If I tell Sam I'm genuinely excited about where this is going, then there's a version of me that's excited about where this is going, and that version can be disappointed, can produce something that doesn't match the excitement, can get to the window in the unnamed city and find she has nothing to see. As long as I keep it in here, it's only mine. The character is only in her room with me and I haven't made any claims about her to anyone.

Riley would have something direct to say about this. Probably something like: you're protecting the feeling by not having it properly. She'd be right in the way she's usually right, which is plainly and a little too quickly, before I've finished building my case.

The other thing I haven't said out loud: I want to finish it. The novel. I haven't written that word down in the context of a thing I'm actively working on, not in the new pages, not here. I've written about the notebook, the finding, the well still being there, the three sentences, the accumulation. I haven't said: I want to write the whole thing. A whole thing with a shape. I want to find out what the character sees through the window and what happens because of it and how it ends.

I want that. The want has been sitting in the room with me and I've been looking at it sideways, the way you look at something bright without turning toward it directly, because turning toward it directly requires admitting it's there and things that are admitted are things that can fail.

I've been doing this with the Mei-situation for two years and I've written about that enough that I can recognize the structure. The thing you want, the refusal to want it fully because wanting it fully is a kind of exposure. The difference is that wanting Mei's good opinion was always partly about borrowed weight, someone else's assessment of my worth. This is different. The character in her room is mine, and what I want is to do right by her, and that's not borrowed from anywhere.

Which might be exactly why it's harder to say.

I've been turning over whether to tell Sam tonight. We'll do the dishes, probably, or sit on the couch with our separate things, and there'll be a moment where the evening gets quiet and he'll be in the room and I could just — say it. I've been looking forward to something in the notebook. I want to see where it goes. The whole way, I think. To the end.

He won't make a big thing of it. That's not how he responds to things. He'll make the sound he makes, the acknowledging one, and he'll probably ask a question or two, real ones, and then we'll go to bed. There's no catastrophe in the telling. The thing doesn't become less mine because someone else knows I have it.

And yet. I keep coming back to the impatience I felt at the table on Tuesday when I stopped writing. The wanting to sleep so I could come back. There's something in that feeling that I don't want examined yet — not because it's fragile, or maybe it is a little fragile, but mostly because it's new and new things have a texture that gets rubbed smooth if you handle them too much in the early days. I want to sit with it for a little longer by myself before I bring it into the room.

I think I'll tell him next week. After I've written a few more sessions, after the window has a view, after I know a little more about what I'm looking forward to and can say it with some specificity rather than just: a feeling, a character, a thing I want that I haven't wanted like this in a long time.

That seems like the right shape of protection. Not indefinite. Just long enough.

The notebook's on the table. Tomorrow morning, before the walk or maybe instead of it, I'm going to sit down and find out what she sees.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been given credit for recently that you weren't sure you deserved — walk me through the moment and what you did with the praise.
entries.content:
Friday morning I found out what the character sees through the window. That's not what this is about but I've been carrying it around all day like a small warm thing in my pocket, and I needed to say it first.

---

Thursday afternoon. End-of-week wrap, the loose kind, just the project team — Mei, Devon, the two people from the adjacent teams we've been coordinating with. Fifteen minutes, meant to be quick. Mei was summarizing where things stood going into the next phase, and she said something about how the groundwork had been laid well, and then she looked at me specifically and said the coordination work over the last few months had been mine and it showed, and she said it simply, matter-of-factly, the way she says things that she means without needing to negotiate whether you'll accept them.

Everyone looked at me briefly the way people do when someone gets named. Devon made a small appreciative sound. I said something like: it was collaborative, I had a lot of help from everyone here. And moved on.

Which was partly true. It was collaborative. Devon made things easier, the adjacent-team people were good to work with, Mei herself had created the conditions that made coordination possible. But it was also mine in a real way — I'd run the difficult rooms, I'd held the threads, I'd sent the note about the reporting structure when the easier thing would have been to let it stand. The collaborative answer wasn't a lie. It was also a reflex, the familiar one, and I knew it while I was saying it.

What I'm less sure about is the part underneath. Whether I deserved the credit in the way Mei meant it.

Here's the thing I keep circling. The work Mei was naming — the coordination, the groundwork — a lot of it happened during the period when I was still running the full performance. The monitoring, the Mei-behind-the-sternum, the yes-before-thinking. I was good at the rooms because I'd spent two years trying to be indispensable as a hedge against the fear that she'd look at me one day and downgrade her opinion. The skill is real. The motivation underneath it was — not exactly pure. More like: I learned to do a thing well partly because doing it well was my strategy for managing something that scared me.

So when she named it on Thursday, I sat there and received it, and I also sat there thinking: but you don't know why I got good at it. You're praising the output without knowing what was running the engine.

I've been turning that over since and I think it might not be the right way to look at it.

Sam asked how the meeting went, Thursday evening, the usual question. I told him about the Mei moment and he said: that's good, she doesn't say things she doesn't mean. I said I know. He waited. I said: I gave the collaborative-effort answer. He said: yeah, you usually do.

I said: I don't know if I deserved it the way she meant it.

He looked up. He said: what way did she mean it.

I said: like it came from — the real version of me, not the one who was doing it for the wrong reasons.

He was quiet for a second. Then he said: is there actually a version of you that did the work and a separate version that was scared while doing it? Or is that the same person?

I didn't answer right away. He went back to his book, which meant he wasn't waiting for me to answer immediately, just leaving it in the room.

I've been sitting with it since. The same person. That's probably the true version. I learned to hold the difficult rooms through years of being in them and being afraid in them, and the fear didn't corrupt the skill, it was just present alongside it. Most things get learned that way — through some combination of genuine interest and anxiety and not being able to stop. The deck was good even though I said yes before my brain had any say. The coordination was real even though part of what drove it was the installed Mei behind my sternum running her assessments.

The monitoring has quieted since. That's real too. And maybe there's something uncomfortable in the fact that the skill got built in the earlier conditions and is now running with different fuel. Like: would I have gotten good at it if I hadn't been afraid? I don't know. Maybe not as fast.
