But the good-at-it doesn't become retrospectively fraudulent because I understand its origins better now.

I think that's what I've been trying to get clear about. Not whether I deserved the praise at all — I did the work, that part's straightforward — but whether there's an asterisk on it because of what was underneath. And the thing Sam said keeps coming back: the person who was scared and the person who did the work are the same person. The asterisk doesn't actually stand apart from the thing. It's just part of what the thing was made of.

What I did with the praise, practically: said the collaborative answer, went back to my desk, thought about it for a day and a half. Did not seek Mei out to elaborate on my response, did not add a qualifying caveat in a follow-up message, did not bring it to Devon for processing. Let it sit. That might be the most important part — the not-doing-anything-additional, the not rushing to either fully accept it or complicate it.

There was a moment on the walk home when I tried the other version. Just in my head: *Mei said the coordination work was mine and it showed.* Saying it plain, without the yes-but and the asterisk and the collaborative-effort hedge. Seeing how it fit.

It fit. That's the honest thing. It was uncomfortable in the way something fits when you're not used to wearing it, but it fit.

The character sees a market through the window. The unnamed city has a covered market with colored awnings and she's looking at it from a room above it and she doesn't know yet if she's just arrived or if she's been there long enough to have forgotten arriving. I haven't decided which. Both seem true for where she is in her particular situation, and I think I'm going to let it stay both for now.

I told Sam about the window tonight. Just that part, not the whole character, not the setup — just: I found out what she sees, there's a market, I'm happy about it.

He looked up from his book. He said: is it good?

I said: I think so.

He nodded. He said: good.

And that was it, one word, the same one he gave me after the phone call with my mom. But this time I held it for longer. Let it be what it was without reaching for the asterisk.

I think I'm getting a little better at that.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been doing longer than anyone around you knows — and when did you last catch yourself deciding not to mention how far in you actually are?
entries.content:
The market has a name now. That happened this week, somewhere in Tuesday's session, without my planning it — the character was thinking about directions she'd been given by someone she half-trusted and the name of the market came up in the giving of them, and I wrote it down, and it was right, and the scene kept going for another twenty minutes past where I'd expected to stop.

I haven't told anyone the name. Not Sam, not Riley. Sam knows about the market, the window, the character and roughly what she's doing in that city. Riley knows the notebook came back out of the closet and that the well is still there. Neither of them knows that in the last five weeks I've written sixty-something pages, that there are now three characters where there used to be one, that the shape of the thing has started to declare itself in the way shapes do when you've been in proximity to them long enough — not planned, just visible, the way a form comes out of fog.

Sixty pages. I've been counting, which I didn't do in my mid-twenties, or maybe I did and that number felt different then. Now it feels like evidence. Something to hold against the version of myself who stood in front of a closet two months ago wondering if the well had gone dry.

The last time I almost said it: last night. Sam and I were at the kitchen table, both of us with our respective things, which has become a shape we slip into easily, a comfortable parallel. I had the notebook open. He was on his laptop. At one point he looked over — not intrusively, just the glance you give someone when you've been in a room together for a while and you're checking the ambient quality of things — and he said: *going well tonight?*

I said: yeah, I think so.

And the fuller version, the one I didn't say: I think I know how it ends. Not certainly — it could shift, it's shifted before, the character keeps doing things I didn't expect which is the whole point — but there's a shape I can see from here, and it's the first time since I found the notebook that I've had that sense, the sense of the whole thing being locatable somewhere ahead of me rather than just possible in theory.

I closed my mouth and went back to writing.

Part of what keeps it private is the same thing that kept the looking-forward private a few weeks ago: new things have a texture that gets rubbed smooth if you handle them too much. I've said that before and I still believe it. There's a version of talking about the sixty pages that turns them into a thing I've done rather than a thing I'm doing. The past tense is a kind of conclusion, and I'm not at the conclusion.

But I don't think that's all of it.

The other part — the part I'm less comfortable with — is that I don't know what it means yet that this is happening alongside everything else. The next phase starts in January. Mei named the coordination work on Thursday. I have a full version of my job, real and substantive, using skills I've actually built, and I also have the notebook on the table and sixty pages and a character who now stands in a named market in a city she may or may not have just arrived in. Both of those things are true at the same time, as I said I wanted them to be, as I said I was trying to let them be without forcing them to resolve.

And mostly they do coexist, better than I'd expected. The writing happens in the evenings, sometimes in the mornings if I wake up early enough, the before-work walk occasionally traded for the before-work session which turns out to also work. I haven't had to choose. But I've also been keeping the magnitude of the notebook separate from the work-world, and I've been telling myself it's because they belong to different registers of my life, the private and the professional, and that's true. But I think there's also something smaller and less dignified operating, which is: if I tell the work-world version of myself how far in I am, I'll have to account for it somehow. It'll become a thing I'm maintaining alongside the professional shape rather than a thing that exists purely on its own terms.

The installed Mei, the one behind my sternum — she's quieter than she was, but she's still there in some form, still noting things. And I think she'd have an opinion about sixty pages. About finding out how it ends. About the question of where that puts me in the taxonomy of people who are serious about things versus people who want to seem serious. I don't want her opinion. So I'm keeping it away from her, which means keeping it away from that whole register of my life, which means not saying it out loud in most of the rooms I spend my days in.

Riley knows more than anyone. She asked last week how it was going and I said I'd been writing regularly, things were moving. She said: *how many pages?* I said: a lot. She said: *define a lot.* I said: more than I expected by now. She said: *are you going to tell me the number.* I said: eventually.

She let that stand, which is what she does. She doesn't chase me into things. She waits.

I'll tell her soon. The number, the market's name, the shape I can see from here. Not because it needs to be reported but because she was the one who asked about the characters like they were real people she might meet, twelve years ago, and she should be the first to know they're real again.

Sam will find out incrementally, the way he finds out most things — not through a conversation where I explain, but through the accumulation of evenings at the table, the nights I come to bed later than usual, the mornings when the coffee is made before he's up because I'd already been sitting with it for an hour. He's been watching the shape of it without requiring the summary. That's its own kind of knowing.

Sixty-something pages. A named market. Three characters where there was one. The end not certain but visible.

I've been doing this for five weeks and no one around me knows how far in I actually am, and last night when Sam asked how it was going I said *yeah, I think so* and went back to writing, and that felt right — not like hiding, more like staying inside the thing long enough to let it be fully itself before I start explaining it.

I'll know when I'm ready to say the number out loud. It might be when I stop counting.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been putting less effort into lately than you used to — not because you gave up, but because somewhere along the way you stopped needing to try as hard? Walk me through when you first noticed the shift.
entries.content:
The Thursday meeting. I keep coming back to it as the clearest example, even though the shift had been happening for longer.

We were an hour in, the kind of meeting where things are genuinely tangled, two teams with adjacent needs that aren't quite aligned, a deadline that doesn't move. A year ago I would have spent the night before running scenarios. Not useful preparation — the circular kind, cataloguing every version of how it might go wrong and practicing responses to things that wouldn't happen. I'd have gone in with my voice already in the controlled-neutral register before I needed it there.

Thursday morning I got up early for the notebook. Wrote for forty minutes, found a small thing that was true about the character, came out of it and made coffee and thought briefly about the meeting and then thought about something else. Showered, got dressed, went to work. Walked into the room with my coffee and sat down.

About halfway through, the moment arrived where someone needed to say the clarifying thing. I said it. The familiar friction, the going-toward. On the other side the conversation opened up, which it usually does when you name what's actually happening, and we got somewhere in the last twenty minutes that the first forty hadn't reached.

Afterward I was at my desk and I realized: I hadn't been managing the pre-meeting the way I used to manage it. The night before was just the night before. The morning was the notebook and the coffee. The meeting was the meeting. Somewhere between last winter and now the whole infrastructure of pre-performance anxiety quietly downsized, and I hadn't noticed because the noticing requires comparing against a previous version, and you stop doing that comparison once the previous version is far enough back.

I think it started with the difficult rooms, the ones where things go sideways. I did so many of them. Not heroically — I was asked to, I showed up, the rooms kept arriving and I kept going in. And somewhere in the accumulation my nervous system apparently filed them the way it files known things: recognizable, survivable, mine. The dread that used to live in the corner of those mornings stopped arriving at its usual size.

The Mei performance is the more complicated version of this and I want to be careful about how I say it because I don't think it's entirely gone, just different. I still care what she thinks. The caring isn't the part that changed. What changed is that I stopped having to expend effort to maintain the performance — the monitoring, the constant peripheral awareness, the checking of her expression for information about how I was doing. That used to take something out of me. Real energy, real attention, real CPU time that was running all day in the background even when I wasn't conscious of it. Lately it just — isn't running. Or it runs much quieter and I'm not attending to it the same way.

I noticed this specifically a few weeks ago. I was in a meeting with Mei and two other people, nothing high-stakes, the routine kind, and about twenty minutes in I realized I had no idea what her face had been doing for the last five minutes. I had been in the conversation. I'd been thinking about the actual content, responding to the actual questions, present in the room I was in. And the monitoring that used to run parallel to all of that — the secondary processing, the constant background triangulation of where I stood in her estimation — was just not happening at full volume. Maybe at all.

It should have felt like relief. Mostly it did. There was a small strange note in it too, something I didn't expect — almost like disorientation, the way you feel when a sound you've stopped hearing was actually providing some structure, and the silence where it was turns out to have its own texture. I'd been running that particular process for so long that its absence was a little noticeable. Not something I wanted back. Just noticeable.

The effort I used to put into the deck — the nights of over-preparation, the practicing transitions until the words became sounds in the right order, the anticipating of every question from every possible angle — I still prepare. That hasn't gone. But there's a difference between preparing because the work requires it and preparing because the dread requires it, and the thing that's gotten quieter is the second kind. I can feel the difference in how I stop. Before, I'd stop because it was past eleven and continuing wasn't producing more, just making the dread more specific. Now I stop because I'm done, because the preparation reached a natural end, and then I close the laptop and go to bed.

The notebook has something to do with it. I've been thinking about why and I don't have a neat theory. But I think the having-something-that's-purely-mine changed the internal math in some way. When the work-world was the only register that mattered, every meeting was higher stakes — there wasn't anywhere else to put the sense of myself as a person doing something real. The notebook exists outside all of that, not evaluated by Mei or anyone, not legible to the professional taxonomy, just mine. And somehow having it makes the professional thing feel like one part of the picture rather than the whole frame. I can do the work seriously without the work being the only evidence that I'm a person who does serious things.

Sam noticed before I said anything. He asked a few weeks ago if something had shifted at work, not accusatorily — he was just reading the evenings, the way I come home, whether I'm somewhere else in my head or actually in the kitchen. I said I thought so, I wasn't sure what to attribute it to. He said: maybe you stopped needing to prove something.

I pushed back a little. Said I didn't think I'd been trying to prove something, exactly. He raised an eyebrow. I conceded the point.

Maybe that's the cleanest version of it. The proving — to Mei, to the installed Mei, to some earlier-formed idea of what it looks like to be a person who is taken seriously — the proving quieted. Not because I got the validation I was after. Or not only because of that. More because I stopped needing external evidence in the same way. The floor accumulated, the way I said before. The notebook, the difficult rooms, the forty-five seconds with Devon, the four seconds with the adjacent-team meeting. Things with weight that didn't derive their weight from what anyone else thought of them.

The effort I put into the Thursday meeting was less than I used to need to put in. The meeting went fine, went better than fine in the important stretch at the end. The deficit between the effort and the outcome was not nothing. I kept looking for where I'd cut corners and I couldn't find them.

You can just get better at things. Not through trying to get better at them, but through time and rooms and accumulation. That's something I know about but keep being surprised by when it turns out to apply to me.

The notebook has sixty-something pages and a named market and a shape I can see from here. The meetings are real work that I'm actually good at. Both things in the same life, not requiring resolution.

What I put less effort into now than I used to: the maintaining. The performance infrastructure. The nightly maintenance of the version of myself I'd decided other people needed to see.

It turns out the maintenance was the thing that was costing the most. And I stopped needing it gradually, without deciding to, until one Thursday morning I woke up and wrote about a character and made coffee and went to work and it was just a day.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been giving yourself less credit for than the people around you are — and when did you last catch yourself correcting someone who said something good about you?
entries.content:
The Mei thing, then Devon, then Sam, and now my mother, apparently, who said *you sound like yourself lately* three weeks ago and I'm still carrying it around like a stone in my coat pocket, taking it out to look at and putting it back.

Friday afternoon, end of the day, Devon caught me in the hall with his coat already on. He said: "you know January is going to go well, right?" Not phrased as a question, the way he sometimes does — just stated, offered. He said: "I've been watching you in these rooms for two years and I think you're the right person for this and I think it's going to go well."

I said: "I appreciate that. It's a lot to figure out, the new structure and the coordination piece, but I think we've laid decent groundwork."

He looked at me for a second. He said: "that's not what I said."

Then his phone rang and he was gone.

I stood in the hall with my bag and thought about what he'd said versus what I'd answered. He'd said: *you.* I'd redirected to: *we, the groundwork, the structure.* He'd said the thing would go well because of something in me. I'd returned it as a logistical status update. And he caught it, named it in five words, and walked away before I could do the third-order deflection where I redirect the redirect.

I've been catching myself doing this for months and apparently I'm still doing it. Not the loud version, not the *it's a pretty small group, it makes it easier* sanding-down I identified back when the woman in the hall said the true thing. Something more subtle. I've learned to accept the praise in the beat, to say thank you without immediately locating the asterisk. But then I substitute — I move from *you* to *we*, from the personal to the situational, from *here is a thing about who you are* to *here is a status report on conditions that happened to produce a decent outcome.* It's a more refined version of the same motion. I've just gotten better at disguising it as collaboration and humility.

The thing is, I know it's not only deflection. The groundwork really was collaborative. January really does depend on conditions beyond me. I'm not wrong about any of that. But Devon wasn't asking me to dispute it. He was saying something simple, a true thing in passing, and I turned it into a project update.

I told Sam this at dinner and he did the thing I'd expected, which was to not look surprised. He said: "what would it have cost you to just say thank you?"

I said: I did say thank you. He said: "before the but." I said there wasn't a but. He said: "the *it's a lot to figure out* was a but." I thought about it and conceded that yes, the *it's a lot to figure out* was a but.

He wasn't being sharp about it. He was being accurate, which with Sam is usually indistinguishable from gentle.

I asked him when he'd last seen me take a compliment cleanly, without the follow-up amendment. He was quiet for a moment, actually thinking. He said: "when you told me the notebook was going well." He said: "you said *I think so* and you meant it and you didn't walk it back."

Which is true. And I've been sitting with the difference since.

Something about the notebook is outside the system. Outside the professional taxonomy, outside the territory that Mei's opinion governs, outside the space where I'm being evaluated against some standard I helped construct. When Sam asks how it's going and I say I think so, there's no one I'm managing the answer for. The *I think so* is just — true. Unweighted by what it might mean for how I'm being perceived.

Whereas everything Devon said on Friday happens inside the system. And inside the system I apparently still correct people who say something good. Even when I'm trying not to. Even when I've made progress on exactly this. The correction just migrated from the obvious version to the more sophisticated one, from the small-group-size answer to the collaborative-effort answer, and I spent several months feeling like I'd solved the problem when I'd really just moved it.

I don't think this means I'm back where I started. I want to say that clearly, here, to myself, because otherwise it sits there and becomes evidence for the worst interpretation of the pattern. I held the four seconds in the meeting. I received what Mei said Thursday without reaching immediately for the asterisk. I bought the ticket before making coffee, I held the reporting-structure position, I called Riley when I said I would. Those things happened and they're not nothing and I'm not undoing them.

But Devon stood in the hall and said *you* and I said *we* and he caught it and I didn't catch it until he was already gone.

What I'm trying to figure out is whether this is the same old thing with a new face, or whether it's something smaller — a habit that's in the process of being replaced but hasn't fully finished. I don't think habits finish. I think they just become less automatic over time, which means you catch them sometimes, and sometimes you don't, and the measure of progress is the gap between the versions, not whether the thing ever stops appearing.

Tonight at the table with the notebook I kept thinking about what Devon actually said. *I've been watching you in these rooms for two years and I think you're the right person for this.* He's not an effusive person. He says things that are true in the Devon way, light and specific, and he doesn't say them because he thinks you want to hear them. He says them because he's noticed something real and he thinks you should know.

I let myself sit with it for a few minutes without amending it. Just: someone who's been in the rooms with me for two years looked at me and said *you're the right person for this* and he doesn't say things he doesn't mean.

I held it for longer than a block and a half. Longer than I've held most things.

Then I wrote for an hour about the market and the character and a scene I hadn't planned that turned out to be the one I'd been building toward for weeks without knowing it. I stopped not because I ran out but because the scene was done and I wanted to sit with it before I kept going.

That's where I am tonight. Devon said the true thing and I redirected it and he caught me and I'm still thinking about it five hours later, which might itself be the progress — that the catching happened at all, even after the fact, even from across the distance of a retreating conversation.

Next time I'll try to take the *you* as *you.*

I'm getting there. Just not in a straight line, which I probably should have expected.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been wanting to do that you've been quietly waiting to feel more ready for — walk me through the last time the window was open and you let it pass.
entries.content:
The window was open on Sunday morning and I let it close and I've been thinking about it since Wednesday, when I finally understood what I'd done.

Sam was out. The apartment was doing its good-quiet version of itself. I'd been in the notebook for about an hour, the scene after the market scene, which is the one I've been orbiting for two weeks — I know something happens in it, something that matters to the shape of the whole thing, but every time I approach it I seem to write around the edges and stop. Sunday I got closer than I had before. There was a moment where I could feel it, the actual thing, right at the edge of the next sentence. The character was about to do something I hadn't planned and the doing of it would require me to know something about her I hadn't fully worked out yet.

I put the pen down. Got up and made more coffee. Stood at the sink for a few minutes looking at the window above it. Came back. Read back three paragraphs. Adjusted a word. Looked at the sentence I needed to write. Got up again and tidied the table around the notebook for no reason.

By the time Sam came back I had written around the scene again and stopped, and the notebook was closed, and I was doing something on my laptop that had nothing to do with anything.

I told myself I wasn't ready. That I needed to understand the character better before I could write that particular moment. That it would be worse to write it badly than to wait.

I've been saying this about the scene for two weeks. Every session I circle it and find a reason to approach from a different angle. The angle-finding is getting elaborate enough that I should have recognized it sooner, but I didn't until Wednesday evening when I was walking home from work and I thought about Devon in the hall — *you* versus *we*, the catching-after-the-fact — and then I thought about the scene, and then I thought: oh. It's the same thing. It's the same thing with different furniture.

The scene is the hardest one because it requires the character to be wrong about something she's been certain of. Not wrong in a recoverable way — wrong in a way that changes what came before, retroactively, the way some realizations do. I've known this is what the scene needs to be since somewhere around week three. I've been not-writing-it since somewhere around week three.

The not-ready I've been telling myself is, I think, real in one sense: I don't know exactly how she's wrong or how she finds out or what the particular quality of that wrongness feels like from the inside. I haven't solved it. And I've been treating the not-having-solved-it as the reason to wait, as if the solving will happen somewhere outside the scene and I'll be able to arrive at the scene having already done the work, just writing down what I've already figured out.

That's not how the other scenes have gone. The market's name arrived inside the scene, not before. The thing that was true about the character in Tuesday's session — I didn't know it until I wrote toward it. Every time I've sat down without knowing what was going to happen, something happened. Every time I've tried to solve it in advance, I've stood at the sink looking at the window above it.

So it's not actually that I'm waiting to feel more ready. It's that being ready would mean knowing what's going to happen before I have to commit to it happening. And that's not possible, and I know it's not possible, and I've been protecting against the not-possible anyway because the alternative is to go in without the floor already laid, to write the character into the moment where she discovers she's been wrong, and find out what that looks like while it's happening. In front of no one but still somehow exposed.

I've been thinking about whether the exposure is actually what I'm afraid of or whether I'm constructing that explanation because it sounds true and knowing the shape of your avoidance feels like doing something about it. Possibly both.

Riley called on Tuesday, not me calling her for once — she just rang. We talked for thirty minutes about her thing, the not-calling-it-a-relationship thing, which has apparently become more serious without her deciding to let it be more serious, and she was processing it in her sideways way while I asked real questions. Before we hung up she asked how the notebook was going. I said I'd been having trouble with a scene. She said: *what kind of trouble.* I said: I know what it needs to do and I haven't been able to write it. She said: *so the trouble isn't the writing.* I said: probably not. She said: *what's in the scene?* I told her, briefly — the character, the being-wrong, the retroactive quality of it. She was quiet for a moment. She said: *you know it's going to be fine even if you write it badly the first time, right?*

And I said yeah, I know.

She said: *do you, though.*

I didn't answer. She made a small sound that contained everything she'd decided not to say, which is its own considerable volume, and then we said goodbye.

I've been sitting with *do you, though* since Tuesday.

I don't think I fully believe, at the level where it would actually change my behavior, that writing the scene badly is recoverable. In my head I know: this is a first draft, first drafts are for finding out, nothing is broken by a bad version of a scene, the bad version is often the necessary condition for the good one. I know all of this. I've said some version of it to myself several times in the last two weeks while not writing the scene.

But somewhere underneath the knowing, there's still a version of me for whom committing to a thing means the thing counts. The way I used to commit to the deck and have it be mine and not be able to give it back. The scene I finally write will be the scene I wrote, and if it's wrong, that wrongness will have happened, and I'll have to look at it. Something in me is still holding out for writing it right the first time, which would mean I'm the kind of person who can do it right, rather than the kind who makes a mess and sorts through it.

The window on Sunday was real. I was close to the scene. I got up and made coffee.

Tonight I'm going to sit down and write it badly. Not as a method, not because I've convinced myself it'll be fine — I haven't, fully — but because I've run out of new approaches to circling it and the circling is starting to cost more than whatever the bad version would. The character deserves to find out she's wrong. She's been waiting in that city for two weeks.

The notebook's on the table.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been carrying around from a conversation weeks or months ago — a specific sentence someone said — that you still haven't fully put down? Walk me through what was said, and why it keeps coming back.
entries.content:
Riley said: *do you, though.*

That's the one. I've been putting it down and picking it back up for two weeks, turning it over, setting it somewhere I think I won't notice it and then noticing it again in the middle of something unrelated. In the kitchen waiting for water to boil. On the train, looking at the tunnel wall.

She said it after I told her about the scene — the character who's wrong about something she's been certain of, the retroactive quality of it, the two weeks I spent circling the edge. I said I knew writing it badly was recoverable. She said: *do you, though.* And then we said goodbye and she let it stand in exactly the way she lets things stand, not chasing, just leaving the door open.

The reason it keeps coming back is that it wasn't just about the scene.

I've been thinking, slowly, across several days of not-quite-thinking-about-it, about what it means to know something versus to have the knowledge available for citation. The difference between carrying an idea and actually living from it. I know a lot of things. I know that the monitoring in those meetings costs me attention I could put elsewhere. I know that saying *we* when Devon says *you* is a form of correction. I know that the pre-performance dread has quieted and the floor has accumulated and the well is still there. I know my mother heard something in my voice this fall that hadn't been there in a while. I know all of this. I've written it down. I've said versions of it to Sam and Riley and sometimes, in small pieces, to myself in the mirror without entirely meaning to.

But *do you, though.* That's asking about a different layer.

Last Thursday I wrote the scene. Not beautifully, not with the false-clarity version of knowing where it was going — I sat down and I wrote into it and the character found out she was wrong and it was messy and the wrongness didn't arrive in a clean sentence, it arrived in fragments across two pages, and I didn't know what it was until I was most of the way through it. I went to bed without reading it back. I read it the next morning and it was rough in places and right in the important place, the one that mattered, and I sat at the table with my coffee and thought: okay.

I should feel finished with the *do you, though.* I did the thing. The window was open and I didn't let it pass. I wrote the scene badly and it turned out fine, more than fine, which is exactly what I knew would happen, which is exactly the thing I apparently didn't fully believe until it happened.

And I don't feel finished with it. That's what I'm trying to understand.

I think Riley's question reached further than the notebook. That's what I keep arriving at when I take it out and look at it.

There's a version of this fall I've been telling myself — the version where I found the notebook and called Riley and bought the ticket and said the fumbling true thing to Mei and the floor accumulated and the monitoring quieted and I sound like myself, according to my mother, for the first time in a while. And all of that is real. I'm not walking it back. But there's something the story-of-the-fall version of me knows that I'm not sure the actual version of me, in the actual moment, fully trusts yet. The story is coherent. The living of it is still catching up.

When Mei asks if something is working, I give her the true assessment. When Devon says *you*, I say *we*. I know that the *we* is a deflection and I still produce it. I write sixty-something pages and I don't tell Sam how many until two weeks after I know the number. I hold the character's wrongness at arm's length for two weeks and then write it in one session and it's fine. I keep doing the knowing and the not-quite-trusting in parallel, and I've gotten good at narrating the gap without always closing it.

*Do you, though* is asking whether the narrating counts as the closing. I don't think it does.

I was walking before work on Friday, the good kind of morning, clear and cold, the city with its bare-branch version of itself coming in, and I was thinking about this. About what it would mean to actually trust the recoverable nature of things, not as a concept I could cite but as something I lived from. What would be different. How I'd move through the rooms.

The closest I've felt to it is the notebook. When I'm in a session that's going — really going, the kind where I lose track of time and stop checking whether it's working — I'm not narrating anything. I'm not saying to myself: this is the kind of person who can do this. I'm just doing it. The knowledge and the doing are the same thing for the length of the scene. The *do you, though* doesn't arise because there's no room between the me who knows and the me who acts.

It arises everywhere else. At work, in meetings, in the hall when someone says a true thing. With Sam when he waits with his particular patience and I give him the reportable version. With my mother, on the weekly call, where something was apparently audible that I wasn't consciously sending.

I want the notebook version to be more portable. That's the thing I keep almost-thinking. Not as a method, not something I can manufacture — the notebook only works because it's outside the system, outside evaluation, outside the professional taxonomy. I can't deliberately make the rest of my life feel like that. But I keep wondering if the gap narrows over time. If doing the thing from the notebook-place often enough eventually teaches the other registers something. If writing the character into her wrongness made it slightly more possible to sit with my own the next time I'm in a room where it's available.

I called Riley last night and told her I'd written the scene. She said: *and?* I said it was rough and right. She said: *so do you, though.* Different intonation this time, almost a period at the end rather than a question mark. I said: more than I did two weeks ago. She said: *okay. Keep going.*

That seems about right. The knowing catches up in increments, not all at once, and the measure is whether you're slightly less behind on the last day than you were on the first. 

I'm still carrying the sentence. I think I'll be carrying it for a while.

The notebook's on the table. The scene is written. The character knows the thing she couldn't know before.

I'll try to take that with me into the rooms tomorrow.

entries.prompt: What's something you did recently that you knew, while you were doing it, wasn't quite honest — not a lie exactly, but a version of the truth you chose because the real one felt like too much? Walk me through the moment and what you left out.
entries.content:
The performance review is the thing.

Not the review itself — that was fine, that was good even, Mei said things that were specific and accurate and I received them better than I would have six months ago, without the full recalibration process afterward. What I'm thinking about is one answer I gave, maybe fifteen minutes in, when she asked where I wanted to go from here.

She meant professionally. Obviously she meant professionally. It was a professional conversation in a professional building about professional things. And I gave her a professional answer. I talked about the kind of work I want to be doing more of, the complexity I feel ready for, the ways I think the next phase plays to what I'm actually good at. Everything I said was true. I believed it while I was saying it.

What I left out: the notebook. Sixty-something pages, a named market, a character who finally knows the thing she couldn't know before. The scene I wrote badly and then rightly. The before-work sessions that have started happening more than the before-work walks, and how that trade feels like the right one on the mornings I make it. The fact that when I try to think about where I want to go — really think, not the correct-answer version — the thought that arrives first isn't about Mei or the next phase or being the person she looks at and thinks *her.* The thought that arrives first is: I want to finish the book.

I knew I was leaving it out while I was leaving it out. That's the part I want to look at clearly.

It wasn't a lie. That's what I keep telling myself and it's true, but it's functioning as an excuse and I want to call it that. The professional answer was honest as far as it went. But it went to the edge of the territory that's legible in that room and stopped, and I know exactly where the edge is and I stopped there deliberately. The notebook is on the other side of the edge. I wasn't going to bring it across.

Part of it is just — category. You don't tell your manager in a performance review that what you actually want most is to finish the novel you've been writing at the kitchen table. Not because it's shameful, just because it's not the right room for that particular piece of information. I understand the categories. They exist for reasons.

But I'm trying to be honest about whether category was really the full reason, or whether there was something else operating.

Here's the something else: if I'd told Mei, in that room, that there's a thing running alongside all of this that matters to me more than any of the professional things — not instead of, alongside, this is the distinction I keep needing — I would have had to defend it. Or I would have feared having to defend it. The installed Mei, quieter than she was but still resident, would have noted it. *That's the thing you're spending your mornings on.* And then there would have been this version of me in her assessment, a slightly recategorized version, and I don't know what category that is but it isn't the one I've been building toward for two years.

Riley would say I'm protecting the notebook from evaluation. She'd be right. I kept it out of the room because I didn't want it to become a variable in Mei's calculation of who I am, and I've been doing that since I found it in the closet — keeping the two registers separate, the professional and the private, and mostly I've told myself it's appropriate discretion and mostly I still think it is, but in the review, in that specific moment, I think discretion and self-protection were doing the same work and I wasn't distinguishing between them.

What I actually said, when she asked where I wanted to go: *I want to be doing work that's harder to assess. You said that in an earlier conversation — the thing about false clarity — and it's stayed with me. I think I do my best work in situations where the right answer isn't obvious and I have to find it rather than deliver it.*

She looked pleased. She said that was exactly the kind of thinking she wanted going into January.

All of that is true. I do want that. But it's also a version of *I want what you're already offering me*, which is the most available kind of wanting, the kind that doesn't require me to say anything that lives outside the shared vocabulary of the room.

The real answer, the one that would have required more: I want to do this work well and I want it to be one part of my life rather than the whole frame, and I have something I'm making that has nothing to do with any of this, and finishing it matters to me in a way that's different in kind from how any of this matters to me, and I don't know yet what that means for the larger shape of things, but it's there.

I wasn't going to say that. I knew while she was asking that I wasn't going to say it, and I gave her the partial version, and it landed and the meeting moved forward and she shook my hand at the end and said she was glad to be going into January with me.

In the hall afterward I stood at the window again — the one that looks over the street, the one I stood at after the first real Mei meeting — and the city was doing its December version of itself, grey and particular, people moving fast below. I thought about the character in her room above the market. I thought about the scene I had to write badly before I could write it right.

I'm not going to go back and amend the answer. It was a performance review, not a confession. The categories serve a function and I don't think blurring them would have been honesty so much as a different kind of performance, the conspicuously-self-aware version I've been trying to avoid.

But I want to mark the choosing. The moment where I felt the fuller answer available and moved past it. It's different from the Devon thing, the *we* when he said *you* — that was reflex, I didn't catch it until he was already gone. This was considered. I looked at the fuller answer and I decided, in two seconds, that it stayed on my side of the edge, and then I gave her the partial version and it was fine and the review ended well.

Considered is harder to be easy with than reflex.

Sam asked how it went. I said really well. He said: *she say anything useful?* I said yes, some things about the next phase. He asked if there was anything I wanted to talk through. I said not right now, I just wanted to be home.

He said okay. Made room on the couch. That was enough.

I wrote in the notebook for forty minutes after dinner. Not thinking about the review, not thinking about Mei. Just in it, just the character and her city and the thing that comes after the wrongness, which I'm still figuring out.

The review happened and it went well and the notebook is on the table.

Both things, still. The same evening. No resolution required.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been doing in secret that you'd describe differently depending on who asked — walk me through the last time someone came close to the real answer and what you gave them instead.
entries.content:
Devon asked on Thursday what I'd been up to lately. We were at the coffee station, the midmorning version, both of us waiting for the carafe to finish. Easy question, the Devon kind, genuinely curious and not requiring anything specific.

I said: not much, the usual, getting ready for January.

He said: you seem different lately. In a good way. Less — he made a gesture with his hand, a vague circular motion. Less in your head, he said. Like you're getting enough sleep or something.

I laughed and said I'd been trying to take the walks again, get outside more.

He nodded and took his coffee and that was the whole conversation.

On the way back to my desk I thought: that's three words, *getting enough sleep*, standing in for sixty-five pages and a named market and a scene that took me two weeks to face and forty minutes to write. Three words as the full account. Devon walked away thinking I'd adjusted my sleep schedule.

The notebook thing is starting to accumulate its own weight as a secret, which I didn't anticipate. Sam knows the broad shape of it — the evenings at the table, the mornings when the coffee is made before he's up. Riley knows there are pages, knows the number, knows about the character in her city and the wrongness she had to learn. But even they don't know what the last three weeks have been like. That I've been waking up some days with the next scene already forming, that there are mornings I resent having to put on work clothes because the notebook is right there and the thing I need to write is right there and instead I'm going to spend eight hours in rooms talking about handoffs and stakeholder alignment. That I've started keeping a second notebook — a small one, pocket-sized — to write down things that arrive during the day when I'm not at the table. Sentences. Details. The way a certain quality of light looks in a place I've never been but the character has been.

The second notebook lives in my bag. I've had it for two weeks. No one has asked about it because no one knows to ask.

This is the part where I'm supposed to examine why I'm keeping it at that level of privacy, and I've been doing that examination in pieces all week.

Some of it is the category thing, which I've written about before — the professional register and the private register, the appropriate discretion of not walking into a meeting with my interior life in my hands. That's real. I believe in it. I'm not going to start telling Devon that the reason I seem less in my head is that I found something to be in my head about that isn't about whether Mei thinks I'm exceptional.

But there's something else, smaller and harder to look at directly.

I think I'm protecting it from itself. From what happens when things get named and assessed and located in other people's ideas about me. The notebook has been, since the beginning, the thing that exists outside the system — outside the professional taxonomy, outside evaluation, outside the performance question. When Sam asks how it's going and I say *I think so*, that answer works because he doesn't have a system that the notebook fits into. He's just asking. The answer can just be true.

But lately I've been aware that the more I say about it, the more it begins to acquire the shape of a thing I'm doing, a project with status, something that could be going well or badly, something that could be reported on. And once that happens I'll start to relate to it differently. I'll check on it from outside. I'll assess it in real time the way I assess my performance in meetings — the peripheral monitoring, the is-this-good triangulation. The thing that makes the notebook work is that I'm not doing that inside it. The doing and the knowing are the same thing for the length of a scene. I lose track of whether it's working because I'm working.

I'm afraid of adding a witness. Even a kind one.

Riley is the closest. She's earned the real version and she's asked for it in the particular way she asks — the *how many pages* question, the *do you, though* question, the questions that reach past the surface-true to the underneath. Last week she asked: *are you going to try to do something with it?* Meaning — publish it. Or at least finish it with that possibility in mind. She asked it carefully, like she knew it was a door I might not be ready to open.

I said: I'm just trying to get to the end.

Which is true. But it's also the partial version, the one that stops at the edge of the real answer. The real answer is that the question of doing something with it has been in the room with me for about two weeks and I've been treating it like a piece of furniture I can decline to look at. Because once I admit that I want it to exist beyond me — once I let the wanting go that far — it stops being only mine. It enters the territory where it can be evaluated by people who are not me or Riley or Sam, by people without the context or the care, and I don't know what it does to the writing if I'm aware of that possibility while I'm doing it. Maybe nothing. Maybe a lot. I haven't figured out how to write toward something and also stay inside it in the way that makes the writing work.

So I gave Riley the partial version and she let it stand, the way she does, and we talked about other things.

What I gave Devon was smaller than partial. Devon got *sleep.* There's a whole spectrum between Devon and the real answer and I'm somewhere in the middle of it with Sam and almost at the real answer with Riley and not quite at it even with myself, in writing, which is supposed to be the place where I don't do the partial-version thing.

Here it is: I want to finish it. I want it to be a whole thing with a shape, the shape I can see from here, and I want to have made it, and I want the making to have been real and not performed, and I want to find out whether it's good in the way I think some parts of it might be good, which requires eventually letting it be seen by someone who has no reason to be kind about it.

There. That's the answer I haven't given anyone, including myself, with that much directness.

The second notebook is in my bag. The scene after the wrongness is almost done. There are probably forty pages left, maybe less, and I can feel the shape of them even where I can't yet see the detail.

Devon thinks I've been sleeping better. He's not wrong that something has changed. He just has the explanation that fit in the space available.

The real one I'm keeping a little longer. Not indefinitely. I'm trying to figure out when the protecting stops being useful and starts being the other thing, the familiar thing, the thing I've been slowly getting better at recognizing.

Not yet, I think. But soon.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been meaning to say to Sam — not the big thing, but one of the smaller ones you've been setting aside? Walk me through the last time you almost brought it up and what stopped you.
entries.content:
He asked last night what I wanted to do for New Year's.

Not with any pressure in it — he was making tea, I was at the table, the notebook open but not being written in yet, the kind of early evening where the apartment hasn't decided what it wants to be yet. He said it the way he asks those questions, laying it down and waiting, not already decided himself, genuinely asking.

I said: something low-key, probably. Stay in, or something small. Nothing elaborate.

He said that sounded good to him, and went back to the tea, and the conversation moved somewhere else, and I didn't say the thing that had been in my mouth for about two seconds before I didn't say it.

Which was: I think I want to finish the draft before the end of the year.

Not as a resolution, not as a system — I know better than to frame it that way, I've been here long enough to know that the calendar-object version of a want collapses under its own weight. Just a thing I've been aware of for a week or so, the way you become aware of weather coming in. The shape of the remaining pages, forty or so, maybe a little less. The scene after the wrongness almost done. The ending visible from here, not in complete detail but locatable, the way you can see a house from a distance before you can see its windows.

It's possible. That's what I keep sitting with. Arithmetically, actually possible. If the sessions keep going the way they've been going, if the before-work mornings stay available, if nothing collapses — there are enough days between now and then.

I didn't say it because I don't know what it costs to say it. Whether the saying turns it into an object, a thing I'm either going to accomplish or fail to accomplish, a New Year's project with a deadline and therefore a potential disappointment. Whether Sam, who has been so careful about the whole thing, so consistent in his *going well tonight?* and his one-word responses that contain exactly the right amount — whether even Sam, hearing it, would shift slightly into asking about it differently. Checking on it. Treating it as a thing with a status to report.

The second notebook is in my bag. I've been writing in it on the train, in the small gaps, things that arrive during the day that I don't want to lose. He's seen me take it out occasionally. He hasn't asked what it is. I don't know if he's decided not to ask or if he genuinely hasn't thought about it, and I haven't created the opportunity to find out.

That's the smaller thing I've been setting aside. Not the novel, not the sixty-five pages, not the wanting-to-finish or the wanting it to eventually be seen. Just: I think I want to try to reach the end before January, and I haven't said it to him, and I've been carrying it around for a week in the same pocket as the market's name and Devon's *you* and Riley's *do you, though.*

The reason I keep almost bringing it up is the same reason I eventually buy the ticket before making coffee, send the note, write the scene badly so I can write it right. Because Sam is the person I come home to and he's been in the room for all of it — the finding, the accumulating, the pen on the desk and the notebook on the table and the evenings where I'm still writing when he goes to bed — and keeping the forward edge of it private from him feels less like appropriate discretion and more like the old thing. The version of distance I used to mistake for protection.

What stopped me last night: I could feel the want being small and new. I've written about this, the texture of new things getting rubbed smooth if you handle them too much. The wanting-to-finish felt like that — still close to the skin, not yet hardened into a commitment, still in the register where it's mine without weight. I wasn't sure I was ready to put it down on the table in front of another person and watch it become a thing with a shape that could be tracked.

But I've been saying that for weeks about various versions of the same thing. And the tracking hasn't ruined any of it. Riley knows the number and the number hasn't stopped accumulating. Sam knows about the market and the market still has its name.

The pattern I keep running into from different directions: I hold things private until they feel solid enough to survive being witnessed, but I can't always tell the difference between *not yet solid* and *afraid of what happens if someone sees it and it turns out to be less than I thought.* The second one masquerades as the first with some frequency. I've been wrong about which one I'm protecting against before.

Tonight he was reading on the couch and I was at the table and it was quiet in the good way, the both-of-us-doing-our-respective-things version of an evening. I had the notebook open. I got to the end of a paragraph and stopped, looked up. He wasn't looking at me. The lamp was on beside him, the city doing its orange-grey outside the window, the ordinary version of the apartment at night.

I thought: just say it. Not the whole architecture of what it means or doesn't mean. Just the small thing. *I think I want to try to finish before January.* Seven words. He'll make his sound and maybe ask a question and go back to his book.

I didn't say it. Again. Wrote another paragraph instead.

But I'm writing it here, which is something, which is sometimes how I locate the edge of readiness — when it shows up on the page without resistance, without the full intricate avoidance infrastructure, I'm usually closer than I thought.

Tomorrow morning, if I get up early, I'll write whatever the next scene is. And when he asks how it went — he usually asks, eventually, in the easy way — I think I'll tell him about the forty pages and the end that's visible and the wanting to reach it before the year changes. Not as a declaration, not as a thing to be tracked. Just something true, said in the kitchen while the coffee brews. 

The way the real things have been going lately: out before I've managed them, landing smaller than the dread that preceded them, the world staying intact.

I think I can do that tomorrow.

entries.prompt: What's something you used to do regularly that you've quietly let go of — not as a decision exactly, just something that stopped happening? Walk me through the last time you did it and when you realized it wasn't coming back.
entries.content:
I told Sam this morning. Before the coffee, which felt important for reasons I can't fully explain except that it's the rule I made for myself with things I keep almost doing — before the coffee, so the small ritual of the coffee can't absorb the moment. He was in the kitchen already, standing at the counter in his sweater, and I said: I think I want to try to finish the draft before the end of the year. He made his sound, the acknowledging one, and he said: how many pages? I said forty or so, maybe a little less. He said: okay, yeah. And then he poured his coffee and handed me mine and that was the whole thing, and the world stayed intact, exactly as predicted.

This isn't about that either. But it needed to be said first.

---

I was looking for a book this afternoon and found the sketchbook. Not the notebook — the actual sketchbook, the large one with the black cover and the spiral binding, shoved sideways on the lower shelf behind some paperbacks. I pulled it out without meaning to, and it fell open to something I'd drawn — I want to say three years ago, maybe four. A window. A fire escape. The view from an apartment I don't live in anymore.

I used to draw. That's the thing that stopped.

Not professionally, not with any ambition attached to it — just the habit of it, the putting-marks-on-paper version that wasn't writing. I had the sketchbook and a small set of pencils in a canvas case that I'm almost certain is in the same drawer as the batteries, and for a period of a few years I drew things. Mostly small things: the coffee cup on the table, the corner of a room, a person on the train who didn't know I was drawing them. Nothing that required skill so much as attention, a kind of looking that slowed everything down to the speed of the hand.

I remember the last time clearly, which is strange because I didn't know at the time it was the last time. I was at the kitchen table — not this kitchen, the one before — and I was drawing the plant on the windowsill, which was dying in a slow and interesting way, the leaves going translucent at the edges. I remember thinking the dying plant was actually easier to draw than the healthy one because the forms were more complicated, less predictable. I remember being absorbed in it the way the notebook absorbs me now: the doing and the knowing the same thing, no secondary monitoring, just the looking and the marking.

And then something happened — Sam called me from another room, or there was a thing I'd forgotten to do, or just the ordinary interruption of living with another person in a small space — and I closed the sketchbook and put it down and didn't pick it back up. The pencil case is in the battery drawer. The plant died. We moved to this apartment. The sketchbook went on the lower shelf.

I didn't decide to stop. I just kept not starting again, which is a different thing, a more gradual thing, a thing that doesn't announce itself as a decision and therefore never gets examined as one.

Standing at the shelf this afternoon with the sketchbook in my hands, I had the thought that always comes with these discoveries: *oh, you're still here.* The way I said it about the notebook, about the cardigan, about the want with some heat in it that turned out to be current. Some version of the same recognition, same mild surprise at what I'd been storing.

But then something else, quieter. A question I don't usually ask in these moments, which is: does it need to come back?

The notebook came back because the want was still current, because I touched it and felt the heat and eventually said out loud I want to stay home and look for it. The cardigan is still in the closet because I keep checking whether I'm still the person who deserves to keep it. But the sketchbook — I held it for a few minutes and I tried to find the heat and I found something softer. Not cold exactly. More like the warmth of something that was good and is now finished, that completed its function in a particular period and doesn't need to be retrieved to have mattered.

I don't know if that's true or if it's the thing I'm telling myself to avoid the work of starting again. I've been wrong about that distinction before, in both directions.

What I know is that the sketchbook represents a kind of attention I used to give to the visible world — the slow looking, the translation of the seen thing into a mark — and I don't think I stopped giving that attention when I stopped drawing. I think it went somewhere else. The October light going flat and gold in the kitchen. The city's orange-grey through the curtain at night. The market with its colored awnings in a city the character doesn't know the name of yet. I've been paying that attention. I just stopped recording it with a pencil.

Maybe some things migrate rather than end. The attention moves from one container to another and the first container sits on the lower shelf, not urgently needed, not quite gone.

Sam saw the sketchbook on the table when he came home. He said: haven't seen that in a while. I said no. He picked it up and looked at the window drawing, the fire escape, and he said: that's the old apartment, isn't it. I said yes. He turned a few more pages without saying anything, just looking, and then he put it back on the table gently, the way you set something down when you understand it's not yours to judge.

He didn't ask if I was going to start drawing again. He seemed to understand that wasn't the question. He just said: I remember that plant, and went to make dinner.

I kept the sketchbook on the table. Not with the notebook — on the other side, the two of them at opposite ends. I don't know what I'm deciding by leaving it there instead of putting it back. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'm keeping it where I can see it for a while, the way I kept the pen on the desk before I understood what I was working up to.

Or maybe it's just a thing that was good, that I'm glad I did, that the apartment can hold for a little while before going back on the shelf.

The drawing of the dying plant is the last page with anything on it. The rest of the sketchbook is blank.

That's a lot of room for something. I'm not sure yet for what.

entries.prompt: What's something someone did for you recently that you didn't thank them for the way you meant to — walk me through what happened and what you did instead of saying the thing?
entries.content:
The sketchbook is still on the table. Three days now, both ends of the table occupied, the notebook on my side and the sketchbook on Sam's and neither of us has moved either one.

Wednesday. That's when it happened.

I'd been in a difficult stretch with the draft — not blocked exactly, more like what I imagine the last mile of something feels like, where the ending is visible but every sentence feels like it's carrying the weight of everything that came before it. I'd been at the table for an hour without producing much, writing and deleting and writing again, which I don't usually do, I usually trust the forward motion and fix things later. I was tired in the particular way that comes from being close to something rather than from exertion.

Mei sent a message in the late afternoon. Just a link, no text, to an article about a kind of structural facilitation I'd mentioned once in passing months ago, probably in the context of one of the difficult rooms. I don't even remember saying it. She remembered. The article was good — specific, rigorous, the kind of thing I'd have found on my own eventually but might have taken another six months to find.

I wrote back: *thank you, this looks great.*

I sent it and then I sat with the sent message for a moment and felt the gap between it and what I'd actually meant to say.

What I meant: that she'd been paying attention to something I'd said in passing, enough to hold onto it and send it when the relevant thing surfaced, and that the paying-attention was the actual gift, not the article. That I'd spent two years performing toward her opinion and she'd been — quietly, without any ceremony — noticing real things. That I don't take that for granted anymore, or I'm trying not to. That the before-Mei version of me would have found the article a week from now and read it alone and it would have been fine, and the having-it-sent is different from that in a way I can't quite account for except that it made me feel less solitary in the work.

*Thank you, this looks great.* That's what I said instead.

I know why. Because anything more would have required me to say something about what the two years have been like from my side, which is not a conversation I know how to have in a Slack message or possibly at all, and because Mei sent the link on her way to something else and didn't intend to open a door, she just saw the thing and thought of me and sent it, and that's its own form of generosity that doesn't need to be made complicated.

But I've been thinking about it since, the gap between the sent message and the thing underneath it, and I want to look at it clearly.

There's a category of things I receive well now that I didn't receive well before. Devon's *you*, mostly — I'm getting better at not returning it smaller than it came. My mother's *you sound like yourself lately*, which I've been carrying like a stone in my coat pocket and warming with my hand. Sam's one-word responses that contain exactly the right amount, which I've learned to let be the whole thing rather than reaching for more.

Mei is different. She's still the one it's hardest to receive from cleanly, and I think I know why: she's the person I spent two years building an entire performance apparatus around, and even though the apparatus has mostly come down, she's still associated with the register where I'm evaluated, where things count in the professional sense, where taking something simply and saying what it meant might feel like giving something away. Or might look like I have feelings about my work situation, which — I do, I've had an intricate and well-documented relationship with my feelings about my work situation, but I don't need to demonstrate that in a Slack message to my senior colleague.

So I said *thank you, this looks great* and closed the message thread and went back to the draft.

The draft moved better after that, which surprised me. I got through two pages that had been resisting, a scene that required the character to receive something from someone she didn't expect to receive it from, and I think the Mei thing was in the room with me while I wrote it. The character doesn't have the right words either. She says something practical and means something larger, and the person who gave her the thing knows this, and neither of them makes more of it than the moment asks for. I wrote that part and thought: yes, that's right, that's how it actually goes, and felt the specific satisfaction of writing something true that I'd come to understand only by being in the middle of writing it.

I told Sam about the Mei message over dinner. The link, the what I wrote back, the what I'd meant. He listened without the patient face — the other face, the more direct one, the *I know what's happening* one. He said: "would she have wanted to know the longer version?"

I thought about it. Honestly considered it. Mei is direct. She doesn't perform and she doesn't require performance. She sent the link because she thought of me, simply, and moved on. The longer version might have been welcome. It also might have been more than the moment was asking for, and there's a version of saying the fuller thing that's more about my need to have said it than about her need to hear it.

I said: maybe. Probably not in Slack.

He said: "there'll be another moment."

Which is the right thing and also, knowing him, a gentle way of saying: you don't have to say everything in the moment it arrives. The having of the thing and the saying of it can be separated. You can carry the gratitude until there's a room for it.

I've been wrong about that timing, historically — holding things too long, until they've lost their currency or I've found reasons to think they don't need to be said. But I don't think this is that. I think this is something I'm still working out how to say, and it'll find its moment, and in the meantime the not-saying is different from the avoiding. It's just waiting for the shape to become clear.

The draft has maybe thirty pages left. Maybe a little less. The character just received something she doesn't have the words for and wrote back something small and practical, and she'll figure out the larger thing when there's a room for it.

I know what I want to say to Mei eventually. It doesn't have to be in a message. January is coming, the next phase is coming, we'll be in rooms together, and somewhere in there will be a moment that can hold it.

I'm keeping the sketchbook on the table for now. Still not sure what for.

entries.prompt: When did you last catch yourself being harder on someone else than you actually felt — going through the motions of a frustration or judgment that wasn't really there? Walk me through what you said, and what was actually going on underneath it.
entries.content:
The draft has twenty pages left. I know because I counted last night, which I promised myself I'd stop doing, but twenty felt like a number I needed to confirm.

---

Tuesday morning, the walk. Cold now in the real way, not the October-clarifying kind, but the December version that makes you want to walk faster and also makes the city look better, the bare branches and the low sky and everything stripped down to its actual shapes. I was thinking about the next scene, or not thinking exactly, more carrying it the way you carry something when you're not quite ready to set it down and look at it.

Devon came up beside me about a block from the office. He'd gotten off the train somewhere behind me, he must have been walking in the same direction. He fell into step and we did the few minutes of morning conversation, the easy kind, and then he said something about a person on another team — not someone I work with directly, someone who'd been involved in a handoff that hadn't gone cleanly. Devon was describing a meeting they'd had, mildly frustrated, and he said: *she just keeps circling back to the same point, you know? Like she's not actually listening, she's just waiting to say the thing again.*

And I said: I know, I've been in rooms with her, it's a lot. 

I kept walking. Devon moved to something else. We got to the office and went our separate ways and that was that.

Except I've been thinking about it since, not continuously, but in the way you think about something you said wrong — it surfaces in the gaps.

Because here's what's true: I don't actually find her that frustrating. I've been in two meetings with this person and she was — fine. She had a point she cared about and she was making it, maybe more than once, maybe in a way that slowed things down. But the thing I said to Devon, the *I know, it's a lot* — I performed a frustration I don't really have. I matched his register. He was mildly annoyed and I stepped right into the shape of that without anything behind it, the way you say *yeah* when someone describes traffic even if you didn't have any.

It wasn't spite. I have no feelings about her particularly. That's almost the more uncomfortable thing — if I'd actually had a bad experience and was venting, at least there'd be a real thing behind the words. Instead it was just automatic, frictionless, the social maintenance of agreeing with a friend's mild grievance. Devon wasn't asking me to co-sign anything, he was just talking, and I handed him the co-sign anyway without checking whether it was mine to give.

I've written a lot in here about the other direction — the managing, the performing, the yes-before-thinking. The way I smooth things over to avoid conflict, to protect against the small deflation, to keep surfaces clean. This is the thing that sometimes lives on the other side of that, which is that I'll occasionally manufacture a low-grade solidarity to avoid a different kind of friction, which is the friction of saying *I don't actually know her well enough to have an opinion.* 

The social cost of that sentence is so small it barely registers. Devon would have said *fair enough* and moved on, maybe laughed. He wouldn't have needed me to share his mild frustration. He was just talking. But the sentence that costs almost nothing somehow still went unsaid, and the co-sign appeared instead, and now this person who I've been in two meetings with has a mini-review in Devon's memory that has my name attached to it, and it's not really mine.

What was actually going on, as best I can tell: nothing in particular. I was thinking about the draft. I was cold. Devon is easy and the conversation had its own momentum and it wanted to go in the direction of *yeah, I know, that's a thing.* The path of least resistance through that particular morning.

I notice I'm not being very hard on myself about this. I want to mark that because a year ago I'd have turned two seconds of lazy social agreement into evidence for something — that I'm the kind of person who says things without meaning them, that I can't be trusted with other people's reputations, that the professional performance extends now to performing solidarity for people who haven't done anything. I'd have found the most indicting version and stayed there for a while.

The actual version is: I said something slightly careless because I was in my head and Devon was easy and the conversation was moving. That's a thing that happens. It wasn't targeted, it wasn't mean, it was just — inattentive. The kind of small social imprecision that happens a hundred times a day in every office and most people don't write about.

But I notice the pattern, the one that's been there for a long time alongside the other one: the smoothing can go in two directions. Sometimes I agree to take on something I don't want to take on, say yeah absolutely to the ask I should have deferred. Sometimes I agree to feel something I don't feel, say *yeah, I know* to the frustration that isn't mine. Both of them are the same motion, when I look at it straight — I locate what the other person seems to want from the exchange and I produce it. Frictionless. The conversation moves forward.

I've gotten better at the first direction. The sitting the beat before answering, the no to my mother about November, the hold the position in the meeting. Those have real weight to them now, at least sometimes. The second direction I haven't looked at as directly.

Sam was reading when I got home. I sat next to him and said: I said something today I didn't quite mean, and I'm trying to figure out why. He put his book on his knee. I told him about Devon and the woman and the *yeah, I know.* He was quiet for a second. He said: did you think anything bad about her?

I said: no, not really.

He said: so it was more like — you lent Devon a feeling you didn't have.

That's the right formulation. I've been turning it over since. The lending. Something about it being more transactional than it sounds — like I was spending social currency I'd borrowed, currency that didn't belong to me, to smooth a thirty-second exchange on a cold Tuesday morning. Not malicious. Just imprecise. Just the automatic version of me before I sat the beat.

I don't think I owe the woman anything — she'll never know, Devon's mild frustration will resolve itself, this is small in every external measure. But I want to notice it because the noticing is the thing. Not as evidence for a harsher verdict on myself. Just: there it is. Still doing it sometimes. In the other direction, with less drama, but the same mechanism.

The draft has twenty pages. I wrote three of them tonight and they moved.

Twenty pages sounds like a lot less than it did two weeks ago.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been telling yourself you'll do once the draft is finished — walk me through what it is and how long it's been waiting.
entries.content:
The draft has seventeen pages left. I counted again this morning, which I keep saying I'll stop doing and keep doing anyway, the way you check the clock when you're almost somewhere.

There's a list. Not written down, which is the form it takes when you want to keep something both available and deniable. I've been adding to it since — I want to pin down when. Probably since I understood the shape of the ending, since the finish line became a real thing and not just a theoretical condition. The list is made of things I've been deferring with the rationale of the draft, and some of them are real deferrals and some of them are things I would have deferred anyway, with different rationale, and I've been using the draft as cleaner cover than I deserve.

The one that's been waiting longest: the sketchbook.

It's still on the table, both ends occupied, the notebook on my side and the sketchbook on Sam's. He hasn't moved it. I haven't moved it. We've both been eating dinner in the middle with these two things at the edges like bookends, which is a little absurd, but the absurdity has a quality I don't want to disturb yet. Three weeks now, or close to it.

What I've been telling myself is that I'll open it again once the draft is done. That there's only so much of that kind of attention available — the slow looking, the hand following the seen thing — and I've been spending it in the notebook, and when the notebook is finished some of it might migrate back. I told myself this once and it felt true and then I kept using it as a reason not to touch the sketchbook and it started to feel less like a true thing and more like a tidy arrangement I'd made with myself that served my convenience.

The pencil case is in the battery drawer. I know this because I got batteries last week and saw it there and closed the drawer.

The other thing on the list is the Mei conversation. The real one, not the Slack message, not the *thank you this looks great.* I wrote about this last month — that there'd be a room for it, that January was coming and we'd be in rooms together and somewhere in there would be a moment that could hold it. I still believe that's true. But I've been holding *once the draft is finished* like a permission slip, like I need to have that particular thing completed before I can be present for whatever the Mei conversation is. Which is — I want to look at this clearly — probably not actually the structure. The permission slip is doing work it wasn't issued to do.
