The Mei thing has been waiting since the review. Since she sent the link and I wrote back something small and practical. Since I understood, sitting with the sent message, that there was a longer version I hadn't found the room for. What I want to say to her doesn't resolve into a sentence yet, which is part of why it's still on the list — it's not that I'm avoiding the conversation, exactly, it's that I don't know what the conversation is. I have a feeling about two years of working alongside her that I haven't finished understanding, and the not-finishing means I can't say it cleanly, and the not-saying-it-cleanly is something I've told myself the draft will fix, that finishing one thing will unlock the language for another. Which may or may not be how it works. I've been wrong about the sequencing of things before.

Riley is on the list too, in a different way. We've been talking regularly — she'd probably say we've recovered the forty-minutes-without-noticing version, which is true, we have. But there's a specific thing I've been waiting to tell her, which is that I think the book might be good. Not certain, not ready to defend it — more like a private sense, the kind that lives in the body rather than the mind, a confidence that doesn't have a full argument behind it yet. I've been protecting it from the testing that would come from saying it out loud, the *what makes you think so* and the *in what way* and the gentle but real pressure of having to account for it. Once the draft is finished, I've been telling myself, the sense will have more to stand on. I'll say it when there's more to say it from.

Riley would recognize this immediately if she heard me describe it. She'd say: you're waiting to be sure before you say anything, and you know that's not how you get sure. She'd be right. The certainty doesn't accumulate in advance of the saying. It accumulates in the saying, through the saying, the same way the difficult meeting goes better when you go into it, the same way the scene I'd been circling for two weeks took forty minutes once I sat down and wrote it.

Sam is the other thing on the list. Not a conversation, nothing that requires ceremony. Just — more evenings actually in the room rather than at the table with the notebook. We've found a shape this fall that works, the parallel-doing, the respective things, and it has been good, I'm not calling it otherwise. But I've been half-present in a lot of those evenings in the way that I'm still present enough but there's a thing running in the background, the draft or the thinking about the draft or the counting of pages. Once the draft is finished, the thought goes, I'll be more available. The parallel-doing will become something more like actually being somewhere together.

I want to be careful with this one. It's the one that sounds like the most real consequence of the thing I've been absorbed in, and it's also the one that's easiest to let myself feel too bad about, and I know that spiral and where it goes. Sam has not complained. Sam has made room, consistently, with his characteristic patience and his one-word responses that contain exactly the right amount. The parallel-doing has not hurt us. But I've noticed my own partial-presence and I'm ready for it to be different, and seventeen pages is now a number that has a time attached to it.

That's the honest version of the list: the sketchbook, the Mei conversation, telling Riley what I actually think, being more present with Sam. Four things, not enormous, accumulated quietly over the months since October when I found the notebook in the closet and the pen on the desk and the well still there.

The draft will end, probably this week if the mornings hold. And the list will be waiting, same as it's been waiting. And I'll either do the things on it or find new reasons to defer them, because that's the shape of the thing — the list doesn't go away when the permission slip is finally issued. The permission slip is the thing I've been manufacturing. The list is just the list.

Seventeen pages. The sketchbook still on the table.

Tomorrow morning, before the coffee, I'm going to open the battery drawer.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been saying you don't care about anymore — but that you checked on recently anyway? Walk me through the last time you looked.
entries.content:
I finished the draft last night.

Not dramatically — I didn't know the last page was the last page until I was halfway through the paragraph and felt the closing of it, the way a door settles into a frame. I wrote the last sentence and then sat very still for a moment and then I turned the page, and the page after was blank, and I just looked at the blank page for a while. Sam was already asleep. The city was doing its nighttime version outside. I closed the notebook and put it on the table and went to bed.

This morning I opened the battery drawer.

The pencil case was there, the small canvas one, exactly where I'd seen it two weeks ago before I closed the drawer. I took it out and set it on the counter and went through the motions of making coffee. When the coffee was done I carried both things to the table and sat down.

The sketchbook was still on Sam's end. I didn't open it. I just opened the pencil case and looked at the pencils — six or seven of them, different weights, the ends worn down by different amounts, evidence of a frequency of use that I don't fully remember but that must have been real. I took one out. The 2H, the lightest. I hadn't touched it in four years at least.

I put it back. Then I took it out again and made a mark on the corner of a piece of paper, the paper I'd been using for shopping lists. Just a mark. The way you check whether a pen has ink. The mark was fine. The pencil was fine. It's a pencil, it doesn't expire.

Here's the thing I've been telling myself I don't care about anymore: whether the drawings were any good.

I used to care, under the not-caring, in the way I care about most things I claim to be past. The sketchbook was always described, when I described it to people, as just something I did for myself, no ambitions, not a thing I was pursuing seriously. And that was true. But underneath the true thing was a quieter thing, which was that I was always checking. Looking back at the drawing and comparing it to the thing, seeing where it fell short, cataloguing the ways the mark didn't capture what I'd seen. Not with real harshness — not the way the voice gets about the professional things, the *you got away with it* and the *this is a performance.* More like a low-grade monitoring. An awareness that the drawings were either good enough or not, and a mild disappointment when they weren't, and a certain amount of relief when they were.

I told myself I'd let go of that when I stopped drawing. That I didn't care anymore because I'd never cared that seriously to begin with.

And then this morning I opened the sketchbook to the drawing of the dying plant, the last one, and I looked at it for a long time.

It's good. Or — it's doing the thing I was trying to make it do. The translucent edges of the leaves, the complicated form of the dying thing, the way the light was falling on one side and not the other. Someone looking at it who didn't know me would see a drawing of a plant. Someone who knew me, who knew what I'm like when I'm trying to capture something exactly — they might see that I was paying full attention when I made it. That the attention is in the marks.

I still care. That's what I found when I looked. Not devastatingly — I'm not sitting here undone by caring about a sketchbook I put on a shelf four years ago. But the caring is there, same as it was, the same way the want in the notebook turned out to be current rather than memorial. I checked the sketchbook this morning the way you check something you've said you've moved on from, and I found that I hadn't moved on, just put it down, and the difference is not trivial.

Sam came into the kitchen while I was still at the table with the pencil case open. He saw the notebook closed, which is unusual — I've been writing in it almost every morning for months, the closed notebook has its own meaning. He looked at it and then at me.

I said: I finished last night.

He said: the draft?

I said yeah.

He came over and sat down at his end of the table. The sketchbook was between us. He didn't say anything for a moment, just looked at the closed notebook and the open pencil case and the mark I'd made on the corner of the shopping list. Then he said: how does it feel?

I said: strange. Good. Like when a sound you've been hearing for months stops and the quiet is its own texture.

He nodded. He said: what are you going to do today?

And I said: I don't know. Maybe just be in the apartment for a while.

He said that sounded right.

He made toast and we sat at the table with our coffee and neither of us did anything productive for about forty minutes, which I can't remember the last time that happened on a weekday morning. It wasn't entirely comfortable — I kept feeling the pull of the notebook, the reflexive reaching for what I was supposed to be working on — but he was there and the table was holding both things at its ends, the closed notebook and the sketchbook, and the pencil case was open with the 2H still out.

After a while I picked up the pencil and drew the coffee cup on the table. Not carefully. Just — drew it. The way I used to draw things, small things, without deciding whether they counted. It took maybe four minutes and then the cup was on the paper and I looked at it.

It was fine. Not the dying plant. But fine, recognizable, a cup that was the cup on the table and not some other cup. I put the pencil down.

Sam had been watching, not monitoring, just present. He said: still there.

I said: yeah.

I don't know what I'm going to do with the draft, or with the sketchbook, or with the Mei conversation that's still on the list, or with the feeling I haven't told Riley yet, the one I've been waiting to say from a more solid place. The list is still the list. The permission slip got issued last night somewhere in that last paragraph and this morning everything it was supposed to unlock is still just sitting there, waiting to see what I'll do.

But I drew the coffee cup. It took four minutes. The cup is still on the table, the actual one, alongside the paper one, and I'm the kind of person who still looks at a thing and wants to mark it. I stopped drawing and the wanting didn't stop. I just stopped finding out.

That's what I found when I looked.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been doing out of habit that you're no longer sure you'd choose if you started fresh — walk me through the last time you went through the motions and what, if anything, felt off?
entries.content:
The draft is on the table, closed. Three days now since the last page. I've been picking it up and putting it down, the way you keep checking a door you know is locked — not expecting a different result, just not ready to stop checking.

This morning I did the thing I do every morning. Made the coffee, opened the notebook, looked at the first blank page.

Sat there for about twenty minutes not writing.

The habit of the morning session is so established now that its absence had its own shape, a kind of pressure in the room where the writing used to be. I turned a few pages back into the draft, not to revise, just to have something to do with the time. I read a paragraph I'd written three weeks ago, one of the ones that came fast, and it was good, and I closed it again and looked at the blank page some more.

The habit formed around a purpose and the purpose is complete and the habit is still running. I know this. I've been watching it run for three days, the before-work session that used to mean something and now means the motion of sitting in the chair and holding the pen.

What felt off was specific: I wasn't looking for the character. That's the thing that used to happen, in the transition between the sitting-down and the first sentence — a kind of locating, the way you'd find your footing in a dark room, finding where she was and what she needed to do next. This morning I sat down and there was no one to find. The city is there, the market with its colored awnings, the room above it. But she's done. The thing she needed to do, she did it. The book ends. She's not waiting for me anymore.

I didn't know I'd miss that specifically. The being-waited-for.

I told Sam at breakfast. Not the dramatic version, just: I keep sitting down to write and there's nothing left to write. He said: of course there is. I said what do you mean. He said: you've been keeping a journal for months. I said that's different. He said: in what way. I looked at him. He looked back. He went to get more coffee.

He wasn't wrong exactly. But he also wasn't right, and I've been sitting with the not-quite-rightness since. The journal is — I don't have a clean way to say what the journal is. Not the notebook, not the draft. Something that happens in the kitchen at night or in the morning when I can't find the character, a different kind of looking, less shaped. The draft wanted to be a finished thing and it became one. I don't know what this wants to be and that might be its function.

The habit I'm no longer sure I'd choose is the opening-the-notebook-and-expecting-the-draft-to-still-be-there. Which is not really a habit, it's more like a grief, the small ordinary kind you feel when something that organized your mornings stops needing to be organized. I've been going through the motions of being a person in the middle of something, and now I'm a person at the end of something, and those require different postures and I don't know the second one yet.

Devon asked this afternoon how the holidays were shaping up. Easy question, coffee-station version. I said: pretty low-key, which is what I always say, and then I almost said something else and he was already talking about his plans and the moment passed. What I almost said was something like: I finished a thing last week and I don't entirely know what to do with myself. Which is not a coffee-station sentence, Devon would not have known what to do with it, but I felt it wanting to be said and I noted the wanting.

Riley would know what to do with it. I haven't called her since finishing, which is three days, which is not long, but I've been composing the call in my head and it keeps not starting with the finish. It starts with: I think it might be good. The feeling I've been protecting from testing. I sat with the draft for two hours yesterday, not revising, just reading, and the feeling is still there — warm, specific, the body-knowing kind rather than the argued kind. I don't know when I stopped needing to finish the draft before I could say it. Somewhere in the reading yesterday, maybe. The permission slip keeps getting issued for things I wasn't waiting for it.

The sketchbook is still on Sam's end of the table. The pencil case is next to it now, open, the 2H out from the other morning. I drew the coffee cup and it was fine and then I didn't draw anything else, but I also didn't close the case and put it back in the battery drawer, which is something. Both ends of the table occupied and the middle where we eat dinner.

I want to know what comes next and I don't have enough information yet and I keep sitting in the morning chair expecting the character to tell me. She's not going to tell me. That was the arrangement — I showed up, she waited, we worked — and the arrangement is concluded.

What I'd choose, if I started fresh, is — I've been turning this over since this morning. I'd choose the notebook. I think I'd choose it faster than I actually did, I wouldn't let it sit in the closet for however many years it sat. I'd choose the difficult rooms, the going-toward, the four seconds of not-filling. I'd choose the before-work walks and the before-work sessions equally, in rotation, without making either one a system. I'd choose calling Riley when I said I would, the no to my mother about November, the fumbling true thing to Mei. I'd choose Sam, obviously, the particular kind of patience he has and the particular economy of it, the one word that contains the right amount.

What I wouldn't choose, given the chance to not choose it: the two years of monitoring. The installed Mei behind the sternum. The yes-before-thinking and the managed voice and the sitting-very-straight in my own kitchen on Saturday morning when no one was watching. I wouldn't choose that. But I also can't know whether I'd have found the notebook without the monitoring eventually loosening, without the fall putting pressure on something, without the accumulated weight of the version of myself I'd built making the building of it visible. Maybe you can't just choose the good version without the long approach through the complicated one. Maybe the approach is part of how you get there.

The blank pages in the notebook are still blank. I'll probably come back to them, or to something — not the character, she's done, but something. The sketchbook has a lot of room for something. I just drew the coffee cup. Maybe tomorrow I draw the table.

For now I'm going to call Riley. Not after dinner, not when I feel more ready. I'm going to call her in the next few minutes, before I find a reason to wait, and I'm going to tell her I finished, and I'm going to say what I've been carrying since yesterday's reading, the warm body-knowing kind, the thing I've been waiting to say from more solid ground.

The ground is solid enough.

entries.prompt: What's something you did recently that you want to get better at — not a skill exactly, but a way of being with someone? Walk me through the last time you got it wrong and what you wish you'd done instead.
entries.content:
I called Riley. She picked up on the second ring, which is the only way she picks up, like she was already there. I said I finished the draft and she said *oh thank god* in a voice that meant she'd been waiting for me to say it, and I laughed, and then I said: I think it might be good. I said it the way you say something when you finally stop protecting it — a little exposed, a little fast, like getting through a sentence before you can change your mind.

She was quiet for exactly the right beat. Then she said: *tell me.*

And I did. Not the plot, not the structure — I tried to describe the feeling of reading it yesterday, the warm specific thing, where it was and why I trusted it. She listened without performing listening. She asked about the character, the one in the room above the market, whether she was still the most real one. I said yes, she is, even more now than before. Riley said: *I want to read it when you're ready.* Not when it's done, not when it's published, when I'm ready. She knows the difference.

That part I got right. That's not what this is about.

About twenty minutes into the call, her voice changed — not dramatically, just a degree warmer, a little quieter — and she said there was something she'd been meaning to tell me. She said it had been a hard few weeks. The not-calling-it-a-relationship had ended, or not ended, but become something that required her to acknowledge she'd been wanting more than she'd let herself say she was wanting, and the acknowledgment happened at a moment when it was too late to be useful, and she'd been sitting with it.

She said it briefly, in the sideways way she says things, and I heard the edge under it, the same edge I heard when she talked about the passed-over job thing last fall. The real thing under the passing way of saying it.

And I said: I'm sorry, that's really hard.

True. I meant it. I kept going: I know you had complicated feelings about it from the start.

Also true. She had described the complications to me across several conversations, I'd been listening, I knew the shape of the thing.

And then I said: at least now you know what you actually want, which is useful to know.

She said: yeah, I guess so. And the conversation moved on, to me, to the draft, to what comes next.

I've been thinking about that *at least* since I hung up.

*At least now you know.* I turned it into a lesson with a silver lining before she'd finished saying the thing. She had been sitting with something hard for weeks — the specific particular hard of having wanted more than you'd admitted, of the timing being wrong, of the private disappointment — and I received it for approximately fifteen seconds before I packaged it into something forward-facing and useful. The way you do when you want to help and don't know how to just stay in the difficulty a little longer.

The Mei thing comes back to me here. Two years ago, in the fall, I mentioned to Riley that I'd been passed over for something and she followed up on it. She held it in mind and came back. I didn't do that for her last spring when she mentioned the job thing, and I knew I hadn't, and I told her so, and she said don't be sorry, just don't do it again. I thought I'd learned something from that. And last night when she said the hard thing I lasted fifteen seconds before I handed her the lesson.

What I wish I'd done: nothing, for a little longer. Just been on the other end of the phone while she said it. Maybe asked: how long have you been sitting with it? Or even just said her name the way she says mine — slightly slower, picking it up and looking at it — so she knew I was actually there.

The *at least* is what I produce when I'm afraid of the sustained discomfort. Hers or mine, I'm not always sure which. There's something in me that moves fast toward the useful, the reframe, the thing that turns the hard thing into information you can act on. I do it in the difficult rooms — friction as information, going toward. But the difficult rooms are professional, they have a purpose, the solving is the point. Riley calling from her apartment after a hard few weeks is not a room that needs a solution. It needs someone on the other end who can stay with it.

I've gotten so much better at staying in my own difficulty. The train thought. The notebook in the closet. The scene I circled for two weeks and then wrote badly and then rightly. I've practiced staying, in the journal, in the morning sessions, in the four seconds when I don't fill the silence. What I apparently haven't practiced as much is staying in someone else's difficulty without reaching for the nearest useful thing.

I'm going to call her again this week. Not with the intent to correct what I said — that would make it about the correction, about me having noticed I did it wrong, which is its own version of centering myself in her experience. Just to call. To ask how she's doing with the specific thing, the actual weeks of it, what it's been like in the mornings. To stay in whatever she says for longer than fifteen seconds before I say anything back.

Sam noticed something was on my mind after I hung up. He didn't ask, he just came and sat near me, the ankle-touch equivalent of a presence. I said I'd talked to Riley and I'd gotten something wrong. He said what kind of wrong. I said the kind where you say something true and useful and it lands as the wrong thing because she didn't need true and useful, she needed me to just be there.

He said: did she say that?

I said no, she probably doesn't know I did it. Or she might. She notices things.

He said: so it's something you noticed.

I said yeah.

He said: that's something.

One word, the economy of it, the right amount. I thought about how long it took me to be able to receive that from him without reaching for more, without finding the asterisk. I'm still learning the version with other people — staying without packaging, receiving without the fast forward to the useful part.

The draft is on the table. Riley is in another city having a hard few weeks. The call I'm going to make is not about the draft.

I want to get better at staying in the room when it's someone else's difficulty rather than mine. Not solving it. Not reframing it into something survivable. Just — being on the other end of the phone, holding the weight of it, long enough for the person to feel that the weight has been held.

That's the thing. It's so small and I keep missing it.

entries.prompt: What's the last thing you did for yourself that had nothing to do with the draft — and when did you do it?
entries.content:
The cup of tea I made on Wednesday didn't count because it was adjacent to writing — I was at the table, the closed notebook three feet away, and the tea was just the thing you do when you're pretending to give yourself a break. That's not what I'm looking for.

Friday counts. I think Friday actually counts.

I'd been meaning to go back to the bookshop on the other side of the neighborhood — the one that's not the closest one, the one with the specific character, a little cramped, organized according to some system that only becomes legible after you've been going long enough to learn it. I hadn't been since sometime in the summer. I'd been meaning to go the whole fall and kept not going, the way you don't do things that are purely for pleasure when there's something else pulling at your attention. The draft was always pulling.

Friday afternoon I finished a meeting early and I had forty-five minutes before I needed to be anywhere, and instead of going back to my desk I went out the side door and walked the long way to the bookshop.

It was warm inside in the way old bookshops are warm, that particular combination of paper and insufficient radiator heat that doesn't translate anywhere else. There were two other people in there, one browsing and one talking to the person behind the counter about something I couldn't quite hear. I went to the back section, the one with the fiction, and I didn't have anything specific I was looking for. That was the whole point. I was just going to look.

I stayed for about thirty-five minutes. I picked up maybe twelve books. I read the first page of four of them, which is the test — not the jacket copy, not anyone's description, just the first page, the first page being the only honest information about whether a book is going to ask something of you or just hand you things. Two of the four passed the test in different ways. I bought one of them. The other I put back and I know I'll go back for it.

Walking out with the book in my bag, the cold hitting after the warm, I had a feeling I hadn't had in months. Not quite relief, not quite satisfaction. Something more like: the world exists outside the kitchen table. The world has bookshops in it, and first pages, and a particular quality of radiator heat, and I had been in it for thirty-five minutes without thinking about the draft at all, which I realized only after, on the walk back, when the thought arrived: I didn't think about it once.

I've been in the draft's orbit for so long that I'd forgotten what it felt like to be fully somewhere else. The parallel-tracking, the monitoring, the secondary processing — I'd traded one version for another this fall. The Mei-monitoring quieted and I replaced it with the notebook, with where the character was, with counting pages I kept saying I'd stop counting. Not bad, not remotely equivalent to the two years of professional dread. But still a loop, still something running in the background while I was in other rooms. Friday the loop was off. I was just a person in a bookshop.

The book is on the table now, on my end, next to the closed notebook. I opened it last night and read for an hour. Not efficiently, not toward anything. Just reading. Taking it in at the speed it wanted to go, which is something I remember saying once, months ago, about a different book I'd been slow-going with — just continuing the story at the speed it wanted to go. The speed of other people's sentences felt different from the draft's speed. Slower, which surprised me. Or maybe I've been moving fast for so long that slow felt like a surprise.

Sam asked what I was reading and I told him and he looked it up on his phone — he does that sometimes, looks things up mid-conversation, not to fact-check, just because something's caught his interest — and he said: looks good. I said it is so far. He said: you seem like you're actually reading. I said what does that mean. He said: like you're in it. Not processing it. Just in it.

I knew what he meant. He's been watching me read things all fall in the partial-presence way, the half-here way, and apparently this looked different. I was in the bookshop chair and then I was in the book. That's the whole thing. It's supposed to be the whole thing, that's what reading is, and somehow I'd gotten out of the habit of it being the whole thing.

There's something I've been noticing since the draft ended, something I'm still orienting around. The draft organized the mornings, the evenings, the before-work sessions that replaced the before-work walks, the counting of pages, the forward motion of it. And now the organization is gone and I keep reaching for the next thing — the revision question, the what-comes-after question, the telling-Riley-I-think-it's-good question, the Mei conversation still on the list, the sketchbook with its case open and the 2H out. All of it waiting to be the next project, the next thing with a shape I'm moving toward.

Friday afternoon I wasn't moving toward anything. I was in a bookshop looking at first pages. The world was the width of the book in my hands and the radiator heat and the low voice of the person at the counter. That was enough. More than enough.

What I want to get better at — and I've been writing about this in different ways all week, the staying in things, the not-reaching — is believing that the moments without forward motion are not lost time. The forty-five minutes in the bookshop produced nothing. There's no output. The book is good but it won't help the draft and it won't resolve the Mei question and it won't make me better at staying in the difficulty when Riley needs me to stay. It was just for itself. I went and I was there and I came back.

I have the walk before work and the fifteen quiet minutes mid-afternoon and now apparently the bookshop on the far side of the neighborhood. Things that help and resist the calendar. I keep finding the versions of them that work and then losing them again when something else fills the space, and then finding them again a little later through some sideways route — a finished draft, a forty-five minute window, a cold afternoon with nowhere specific to be.

The book is on the table. I'm going to read more of it tonight, not as a project, not toward anything.

The world exists outside the kitchen table. I keep needing to re-learn that, and apparently re-learning it looks like thirty-five minutes in a warm bookshop on a Friday, coming out with one book in my bag and the cold hitting after the heat.

That's enough. That was always enough.

parts.name: The Deflector
parts.description: Sands down compliments, redirects credit to circumstances or others, and resists owning achievements as exposure.

parts.name: The Fixer
parts.description: Reaches fast for the useful reframe, packages difficulty into lessons or silver linings, and solves rather than stays.

parts.name: The Hider
parts.description: Keeps real wants and the most current self private to protect them from evaluation, witnesses, and the risk of becoming legible.

parts.name: The Inner Critic
parts.description: The harsh internal voice that catastrophizes, polices her worth, and treats normal outcomes as evidence of failure or fraud.

parts.name: The Performer
parts.description: Says yes before thinking, manages others' perceptions of her competence, and maintains a performance posture even when alone.

parts.name: The Postponer
parts.description: Manufactures preconditions, researches instead of doing, and dresses up avoidance as not-yet-ready when something feels exposing.

parts.name: The Quiet Maker
parts.description: The buried creative self carrying the want with actual heat in it — the version of her who got up early on Saturdays without performing toward anything.

parts.name: The Unseen One
parts.description: Carries the painful belief that she will only ever be trusted-but-not-chosen, merely fine rather than exceptional, never quite seen.

highlights.exact:
And I said: *oh, it's a pretty small group, it makes it easier.*

I heard myself say it. I watched myself sand it down in real time, take the compliment and return it smaller, attributed to circumstances rather than to anything I did.
highlights.prefix: t elaborate, she kept walking.
highlights.suffix: She was already gone by the time
highlights.reasoning: Real-time deflection of a hallway compliment

highlights.exact: I think part of me is afraid that if I acknowledge what I'm good at, I'm also agreeing to be accountable for it. Like if I say yes, I'm good at this, then I've made a claim that can be tested and disproven. Right now I can hedge. I can say I think it went okay, I think it was useful, the circumstances helped. That keeps the worst version away — the version where I believed in myself and then turned out to be wrong. Saying yeah, actually, I'm good at this, that's mine — that's a different kind of exposure.
highlights.prefix: and the hardest to write down.
highlights.suffix: Riley would probably identify th
highlights.reasoning: The protective function of not owning skill — fear of accountability

highlights.exact: Something more subtle. I've learned to accept the praise in the beat, to say thank you without immediately locating the asterisk. The Deflector.positiveIntent: Make sure no one I care about feels let down or under-prioritized.
The Deflector.fearedOutcome: Saying the wrong thing — or saying nothing — and losing the warmth of a relationship I depend on.
